title: The Boy Who Went to the Wind's Cave
summary: Little Mats sets out on a perilous journey to the end of the world to find his kidnapped sister. To reach the Wind's Cave and the magical realm where she is held, he must make terrible sacrifices along the way, giving up one ear, one eye, and one hand, each loss enabling him to pass through a different barrier. When he finally reaches the wind's domain, he confronts the forces that took his sister and rescues her from captivity. As a reward, his losses are miraculously restored, and he wins a princess and half the kingdom.
full_text: Little Mats sets out on a perilous journey to the end of the world to find his kidnapped sister. To reach the Wind's Cave and the magical realm where she is held, he must make terrible sacrifices along the way — he gives up one ear, one eye, and one hand, each loss enabling him to pass through a different barrier or gain a necessary boon. His journey takes him through enchanted forests, across impossible mountains, and past guardians that test his courage and resolve. When he finally reaches the wind's domain, he confronts the forces that took his sister and rescues her from captivity. As a reward for his bravery and self-sacrifice, he not only retrieves his sister safely but also saves a princess from the same fate. The magical losses he suffered are miraculously restored to him. In classic fairy-tale fashion, he marries the princess and receives half the kingdom. The story follows the archetypal pattern of the youngest or humblest hero who succeeds through courage and sacrifice where others would fail.
moral: Great rewards require great sacrifices, and true courage is measured by what one is willing to give up.
source: Bland tomtar och troll, 1910 (Alfred Smedberg). English translation in Great Swedish Fairy Tales (Holger Lundbergh, 1973).

source_references.title: East of the Sun and West of the Moon
source_references.author: Asbjørnsen, P.C. & Moe, J. (trans. Theodore Hansen)

title: Brother Martin
summary: Set during Sweden's Christianization, Brother Martin, a monk from Sigtuna, travels into the remote Bergslagen region to missionize among pagan forest dwellers. The deep woods are still ruled by the old beliefs, and he finds himself unable to convert the stubborn, idolatrous people. As he pushes deeper into the wilderness, he is set upon by trolls, elves, and other supernatural beings that guard the ancient ways. Despite his unwavering devotion, Brother Martin is overwhelmed by the hostile forces and suffers martyrdom, receiving the martyr's crown in heaven.
full_text: Set during the time of Sweden's Christianization, this story follows Brother Martin, a monk who travels from Sigtuna — one of Sweden's oldest Christian settlements — into the remote Bergslagen region to missionize among the pagan forest dwellers. The deep woods of Bergslagen are still ruled by the old beliefs, and Brother Martin finds himself unable to convert the stubborn, idolatrous people who worship the ancient gods and spirits. As he pushes deeper into the wilderness, he becomes lost and increasingly isolated from the human world. The forest closes around him, and he is set upon by trolls, elves, and other supernatural beings that guard the ancient ways. They torment him both physically and spiritually, testing his faith. Despite his unwavering devotion, Brother Martin is ultimately overwhelmed by the hostile forces of the forest and suffers martyrdom. His reward comes not on earth but in heaven, where he receives the martyr's crown for his sacrifice. John Bauer's illustrations for this story — particularly 'Brother St. Martin and the Three Trolls' (1913, Nationalmuseum) — show the monk standing amid a press of grotesque trolls while a luminous figure shines with otherworldly light.
moral: Faith maintained in the face of overwhelming darkness is its own reward.
source: Bland tomtar och troll, 1913 (Emil Eliason). Illustrated by John Bauer.

title: The Boy Who Strapped the Troll to the Sled
summary: A boy wanders deep into the forest and comes to the trolls' cave. Because he is not receptive to their temptations, the trolls have no power to hold him and are forced to bring him home. The tale illustrates the folk belief that trolls can only hold power over those who yield to their enticements.
full_text: A young boy, adventurous and unafraid, wanders deep into the dark forest and stumbles upon the cave where a family of trolls dwell. The trolls, grotesque and hungry, try to tempt the boy with treasures, sweets, and promises of power, hoping to ensnare him in their dark world. But the boy remains pure of heart and unreceptive to their temptations. He neither fears them nor desires what they offer. Because he does not yield to their enticements, the trolls have no power to hold him. Bound by their own rules, the frustrated trolls are forced to bring him safely home. The tale illustrates the deep folk belief that trolls and other supernatural beings can only hold power over those who surrender to greed, fear, or temptation. An innocent heart is the best protection against the darkest forces.
moral: Those who resist temptation have nothing to fear from evil.
source: Bland tomtar och troll, 1907 (Harald Östenson). Illustrated by John Bauer.

source_references.title: Trolls and Myths of Northern Europe
source_references.author: Davidson, H.R. Ellis

title: The Boy and the Tomte Cap
summary: A burlesque story about a boy who borrows the tomte's invisibility cap and embarks on hair-raising adventures. With the cap's magic, he rescues an enchanted girl from a troll's power and wins both her hand and great riches. The tale follows the classic trickster-hero pattern common in Swedish folklore where a clever human outwits supernatural beings.
full_text: A clever and mischievous boy living on a farm discovers that the old tomte who guards the barn possesses a magical red cap that grants invisibility to anyone who wears it. One night, the boy sneaks into the barn and borrows the cap while the tomte sleeps. With the cap's magic, he embarks on a series of hair-raising adventures. He wanders into the deep forest invisible, spies on the secret meetings of the trolls, and learns of a beautiful girl held captive by a wicked troll king. Using his invisibility, the boy infiltrates the troll mountain, outwits the troll guards, and rescues the enchanted girl from the troll's power. In classic trickster-hero fashion, he uses cleverness rather than strength to overcome each obstacle. He returns home a hero, wins the girl's hand in marriage, and receives great riches from her grateful father. The story is told with humor and warmth, celebrating the cleverness of the common folk.
moral: Cleverness and courage can overcome even the most powerful enemies.

source_references.title: The Tomte: A Swedish Christmas Tradition
source_references.author: Aurelius, K.A.

title: Beauty and the Beast (Swedish)
summary: Helena Nyblom's Swedish adaptation of the classic French fairy tale. A merchant's daughter sacrifices herself to live with a hideous Beast to save her father's life. Over time she learns to see past appearance and grows to love the Beast, whose love breaks an enchantment and reveals a handsome prince. Nyblom's version appeared in Bland tomtar och troll (1911) with illustrations by John Bauer.
full_text: Helena Nyblom's Swedish adaptation of the classic French fairy tale by Gabrielle-Suzanne Barbot de Villeneuve and Jeanne-Marie Leprince de Beaumont. A wealthy merchant loses his fortune, and his family falls into poverty. While traveling, he loses his way and seeks shelter in a magnificent castle where he picks a rose for his youngest daughter. The castle's master, a terrifying Beast, appears and demands the merchant's life in payment for the stolen rose. The merchant's brave daughter offers herself in her father's place and goes to live in the Beast's castle. Over time, she learns to see past the Beast's frightening appearance and discovers his gentle, kind nature. Through her growing love and acceptance, the Beast's enchantment is broken, revealing a handsome prince. Nyblom's version was published in Bland tomtar och troll (1911) with distinctive illustrations by John Bauer that capture both the terrifying and tender aspects of the story. Her retelling emphasizes the power of inner beauty and the transformative nature of true love.
moral: True beauty lies within, and love has the power to transform even the most beastly appearance.
source: Bland tomtar och troll, 1911 (Helena Nyblom). Nyblom's collection Djur och människor (1914).

source_references.title: The Bear's Son: A Folktale Motif in Scandinavian and German Literature
source_references.author: Holbek, B.

title: The Brooms' Broom, King of All Brooms
summary: Jon, a poor broom-maker living in the forest, decides to bind a magical broom: Kvastarnas kvast, alla kvastars kung. He visits three forest hags for help — Vingmor (flight), Tassmor (bouncing), and Labbmor (striking power). At the Great Mountain, he confronts the troll king, commands his broom to steal his scepter, and rescues lost husbands from the trolls. The broom is sold for enough to build a dream home.
full_text: Jon is a poor broom-maker living in the forest — as poor as a stick but as happy as a green twig. One day he decides to bind a special broom with magical powers: Kvastarnas kvast, alla kvastars kung (The Brooms' Broom, King of All Brooms). He visits three forest hags for help: Vingmor, who fills the broom with 'flight'; Tassmor, who fills it with 'hoppsan' (bouncing/jumping power); and Labbmor, dressed in bearskin with gingerbread eyes and a steaming red mouth, a belt of gnawed skulls and bones, who gives it 'striking power.' At the Great Mountain, Jon confronts the troll king who sits with his scepter. Jon commands his broom to steal the scepter and makes it the broom's handle. He rescues the lost husbands of Vingmor, Tassmor, and Labbmor from the trolls, returns them home, and finally sells the magical broom to a wealthy man for enough money to build a dream home for his family. The broom fills Jon with strength, joy, and good humor for life.
moral: Skill, courage, and a touch of magic can transform a humble life into one of joy.

title: Bishop Svedberg and the Devil
summary: Bishop Svedberg's wagon loses a wheel on the way to Brunsbo but he calmly continues the journey. Upon arrival he reveals the devil himself had been serving as the missing wheel. The devil later burns down Brunsbo in revenge and the bishop banishes him to hell.
full_text:
Bishop Svedberg, of Skara, was a very pious man and a mighty preacher, therefore,
intolerable to the devil.

One night the Bishop set out from Skara to his bishopric in Brunsbo. When he was on
the way some distance, the wagon began to run from side to side of the road, and finally one of the hind wheels fell off and rolled away into
the ditch.

The driver called the attention of the Bishop to this, and remarked that they could
go no farther.

“Don’t trouble yourself about that,” said the Bishop. “Throw the wheel into the rear
of the wagon and we will go along.”

The servant thought this a strange command, but did as directed, and the journey was
continued to Brunsbo without further adventure.

Arriving at the inn, the Bishop directed the servant to go to the kitchen and bring
a light.

“Look, now,” said the Bishop to the servant upon his return, “and you shall see who
has been the fourth wheel,” at the same time springing from the wagon.

The servant turned the light in the direction indicated, where he saw none other than
the devil himself, standing in the place of the wheel, with the axle in his hands.

The devil soon found an opportunity for revenge. One night a great fire spread over
Brunsbo, and before morning the whole place was burned to the ground.

The Bishop was at no loss to know who had played him this foul trick, and called the
devil to account for the devastation.

“Verily, you shall know,” said the devil. “Your maid was down in the pantry, and there
snuffed the candle. Passing by, I took the snuffing and with it set fire to the place.”

The Bishop was obliged to be content with this answer, but in order that the devil
should do him no further harm he sent him, with all his imps, to hell.
moral: A pious man armed with faith can overcome even the devil's mischief.
source: Herman Hofberg, Swedish Fairy Tales (1888), translated by W.H. Myers.

title: Boots and the Troll
summary: A poor boy who is ridiculed for wearing oversized boots discovers that the footwear possesses magical properties that allow him to outsmart a greedy troll. Through wit and the strange power of his boots, he manages to secure a fortune and prove his worth to the community that once mocked him.
full_text: Once upon a time there was a poor boy who had nothing in the world but a pair of boots that were far too large for him. Every time he walked, the boots clapped loudly, and the village children laughed at him. 'Look at the Boot-boy!' they cried. The boy was sad, but he wore the boots because they were all he had. One day, while walking through the deep forest, the boy encountered a monstrous troll who guarded a bridge. The troll demanded a toll of gold, which the boy did not have. However, as the boy stepped forward, the oversized boots made a sound so thunderous and strange that the troll became terrified, believing a giant was approaching. The boy, realizing the troll's fear, played along, shouting in a booming voice and stomping his boots. The troll, convinced he was outmatched, offered the boy all his gold just to be left in peace. The boy returned to the village, no longer mocked, for he had the wealth of a troll and the wisdom to use his flaws to his advantage.
moral: What others perceive as a weakness can become your greatest strength if used wisely.

source_references.title: East of the Sun and West of the Moon
source_references.author: Asbjørnsen, P.C. & Moe, J. (trans. Theodore Hansen)

title: The Big Bird Dan
summary: A tale involving a giant bird named Dan who becomes an unlikely ally to a courageous protagonist. The story explores the relationship between humans and the mythical creatures of the wild, focusing on trust and the breaking of ancient curses.
full_text: There once lived a man who ventured further into the wild north than any had dared, seeking the legendary Big Bird Dan. Dan was a creature of immense size, with feathers that shimmered like the Aurora Borealis and a wingspan that could cover a village. The villagers feared Dan, claiming he stole the livestock, but the man discovered that Dan was actually protecting the valley from a subterranean serpent. The man spent many months winning the bird's trust by bringing it fresh fish and singing songs of the old world. In return, Dan allowed the man to ride upon its back, soaring above the clouds to see the edges of the world. Together, they fought the serpent in a battle that shook the earth, eventually driving the beast back into the depths. The people of the valley ceased their fear and built a shrine to the Big Bird Dan, who remained as the eternal guardian of the North.
moral: Understanding and friendship can overcome fear and prejudice.

title: The Boy Who Had an Eating Match with a Troll
summary: A small but hungry boy is challenged to an eating contest by a massive troll. Using a clever trick involving a hidden bag of food and a misunderstanding of proportions, the boy manages to out-eat the troll and win a great prize.
full_text: Once there was a boy whose appetite was legendary; he could eat more than three grown men. One afternoon, he encountered a troll who boasted that no human could ever out-eat him. The troll challenged the boy to a match: whoever stopped eating first would become the servant of the other. The troll brought out mountains of meat, cakes, and porridge. The boy, knowing he couldn't eat as much as a troll, had secretly sewn a large pocket into his coat and filled it with a magic sponge that absorbed food. As the troll gorged himself, the boy pushed much of his meal into the pocket, appearing to swallow it all. Hours passed, and the troll began to feel sluggish and full. The boy, still appearing hungry, asked for another platter. The troll, overwhelmed and defeated, admitted his loss. He gave the boy a bag of gold and a magical sword, terrified by the boy's seemingly bottomless stomach.
moral: Intelligence and strategy can overcome raw power and size.

source_references.title: The Troll in Scandinavian Folk Narrative

title: Bruin and Reynard
summary: A series of encounters between Bruin the Bear and Reynard the Fox, where the fox's superior intelligence and deceptive nature allow him to repeatedly outsmart the stronger but slower-witted bear, often leading the bear into ridiculous and embarrassing situations.
full_text:
BRUIN AND REYNARD

The Bear and the Fox had once bought a firkin of butter together; snip they were to have it at Yule and hid it till then under a thick spruce bush. After that they went a little way off and lay down on a sunny bank to sleep. So when they had lain a while the Fox got up, shook himself, and bawled out 'yes'. 

Then he ran off straight to the firkin and ate a good third part of it. But when he came back, and the Bear asked him where he had been, since he was so fat about the paunch, he said: 'Don’t you believe then that I was bidden to barsel, to a christening feast.' 

'So, so', said the Bear, 'and pray what was the bairn’s name.' 

'Just-begun', said the Fox. 

So they lay down to sleep again. In a little while up jumped the Fox again, bawled out 'yes', and ran off to the firkin. This time too he ate a good lump. When he came back, and the Bear asked him again where he had been, he said: 'Oh, wasn’t I bidden to barsel again, don’t you think.' 

'And pray what was the bairn’s name this time', asked the Bear. 

'Half-eaten', said the Fox. 

The Bear thought that a very queer name, but he hadn’t wondered long over it before he began to yawn and gape and fell asleep. Well, he hadn’t lain long before the Fox jumped up as he had done twice before, bawled out 'yes' and ran off to the firkin, which this time he cleared right out. When he got back he had been bidden to barsel again, and when the Bear wanted to know the bairn’s name, he answered: 'Licked-to-the-bottom.' 

After that they lay down again, and slept a long time; snip but then they were to go to the firkin to look at the butter, and when they found it eaten up, the Bear threw the blame on the Fox, and the Fox on the Bear; snip and each said the one had been at the firkin while the other slept. 

'Well, well', said Reynard, 'we’ll soon find this out, which of us has eaten the butter. We’ll just lay down in the sunshine, and he whose cheeks and chaps are greasiest when we wake, he is the thief.' 

Yes, that trial Bruin was ready to stand; snip and as he knew in his heart he had never so much as tasted the butter, he lay down without a care to sleep in the sun. Then Reynard stole off to the firkin for a morsel of butter, which stuck there in a crack, and then he crept back to the Bear, and greased his chaps and cheeks with it; snip and then he, too, lay down to sleep as if nothing had happened. 

So when they both woke, the sun had melted the butter, and the Bear’s whiskers were all greasy; snip and so it was Bruin after all, and no one else, who had eaten the butter.
moral: Do not trust those who use flattery and trickery to hide their own faults.

title: The Best Wish
summary: Three brothers each receive one wish. The elders wish for money, but the youngest wishes that every woman he sees falls in love with him, leading to a life of luxury and affection.
full_text: Three brothers are each granted a single wish. The two older brothers wish that they can pull money out of their pockets whenever they like. The youngest, Boots, wishes that every woman he encounters falls instantly in love with him. While the older brothers are wealthy, they are often arrogant. Boots, despite having no money initially, is treated with immense kindness and given magical gifts—scissors that cut silk from air, a tablecloth that produces feasts, and a tap that pours the finest drinks—by the wives of innkeepers. Eventually, he wins the heart of a princess and half a kingdom, proving that affection is more valuable than gold.
moral: True wealth lies in the love and affection of others rather than in material riches.

title: Boots Who Made the Princess Say 'That's a Story'
summary: A king offers his daughter's hand to anyone who can make her admit a story is a lie. Boots wins by telling an increasingly absurd tale of exaggeration that eventually forces the princess to concede.
full_text: A king's daughter is a prolific liar, and the king promises her hand to anyone who can get her to say 'That's a story' in response to a lie. Many fail, but Boots succeeds by engaging her in a competition of exaggerations. He describes a farmyard so large that shepherds at opposite ends cannot hear each other's horns, an ox so big that men on its horns cannot touch with a rule, and a cow whose milk fills vats. He culminates the tale by describing a spruce tree that grew from a mare's backbone, leading him to heaven where the Virgin Mary spun foam into ropes, and finally falling into a fox's hole where the princess's father was being boxed on the ear. The princess, unable to resist, exclaims, 'That's a story!', and Boots wins the prize.
moral: Creativity and a sense of humor can overcome the most stubborn of personalities.

source_references.title: The Types of the Norwegian Folktale
source_references.author: Krohn, J.

title: Boots and His Brothers
summary: Three brothers try to win a princess by felling a magical oak and digging a well. The older brothers fail and are exiled, but the youngest, Boots, uses magical tools to succeed.
full_text: A King offers his daughter and half the kingdom to anyone who can fell a magical oak that regrows and dig a well that holds water year-round. The two elder brothers fail and are exiled to a barren island. The youngest, Boots, finds a magical axe, a magical spade, and a magical walnut. Using these tools, he effortlessly fells the tree and fills the well. He wins the Princess and the kingdom, while his brothers remain on the island, forever wondering why they underestimated the curious nature of Boots.
moral: Curiosity and a willingness to explore often lead to solutions that conventional methods cannot find.

title: Buttercup
summary: A plump and fond-of-good-things boy named Buttercup is captured by a wicked witch. Despite her terrifying nature, Buttercup uses his quick wit and appetite to outmaneuver her, eventually escaping her clutches to find wealth and freedom.
full_text: Once on a time there was an old wife who sat and baked. She had a little son who was so plump and fat, and so fond of good things, that they called him Buttercup. One day, while his mother was baking, a dog named Goldtooth began to bark, and the mother sent Buttercup out to see what was happening. There he met a witch who lured him away. The witch intended to eat him, but Buttercup was a clever lad. He managed to trick the witch into performing tasks for him and used his wit to avoid being cooked. He discovered the witch's secrets and, through a series of clever ruses, managed to escape her hut with a hoard of gold and jewels, returning home to live a life of luxury with his mother.
moral: Intelligence and quick thinking are more powerful than brute force or dark magic.

title: Big Peter and Little Peter
summary: Two brothers of vastly different sizes—one a giant and one tiny—set out to seek their fortune. Though they are opposites in physical stature, they find that their differing abilities complement each other perfectly, allowing them to succeed where others failed.
full_text: Once on a time there were two brothers, Big Peter and Little Peter. Big Peter was as large as a mountain, and Little Peter was as small as a thumb. They decided to leave home to find their fortune. Throughout their journey, they encountered obstacles that only one of them could solve. When a heavy boulder blocked their path, Big Peter lifted it with ease. When they needed to retrieve a key from a narrow crevice, Little Peter slipped inside and brought it back. They realized that neither could have succeeded alone, and by combining their strengths, they outsmarted various trolls and giants, eventually winning great riches and a peaceful life together.
moral: Everyone possesses unique strengths, and true success is achieved through cooperation and mutual support.

title: Bushy Bride
summary: A man marries a woman who appears bushy and strange in form, much to the surprise of his family. However, her outward appearance hides a magical nature and a virtuous heart, which eventually bring prosperity and joy to the household.
full_text: Once upon a time, a man decided to marry a woman known as the Bushy Bride. She was covered in strange, bushy foliage and looked more like a shrub than a human, causing the townspeople to laugh and his parents to recoil in horror. Despite the social stigma, the man saw a kindness in her eyes and remained devoted to her. As time passed, the Bushy Bride used her magical abilities to heal the sick and make the crops flourish. Eventually, her bushy exterior fell away to reveal a woman of stunning beauty. Her virtues had transformed her form, and the man's unwavering acceptance had unlocked her true self, leading to a lifetime of happiness.
moral: Do not judge others by their appearance; true beauty and virtue are often hidden beneath an unlikely surface.

title: The Blue Belt
summary: A protagonist obtains a magical blue belt that grants superhuman strength and the ability to overcome impossible trials. Using the belt, they defeat monsters and solve the puzzles of a distant king to win a great reward.
full_text: Once on a time, a poor youth was gifted a shimmering blue belt by a mysterious wanderer. The belt granted the wearer immense strength and the courage of a lion. With the blue belt fastened around his waist, the youth set out to find the legendary Golden City. Along the way, he fought off giant serpents and moved mountains that blocked his path. Upon arriving at the city, the king challenged him to a series of impossible feats. Thanks to the belt, he succeeded in every task, eventually proving himself to be the most capable man in the land. He was rewarded with a kingdom of his own, but he never forgot the kindness of the wanderer who gave him the belt.
moral: When combined with a brave heart, a tool of power can be used to change one's destiny for the better.

title: Birth of Wainamoinen
summary: In primeval times, the Daughter of the Ether descends to the ocean. A duck nests on her knee, laying six golden eggs and one iron egg. These eggs break, forming the earth, sky, sun, moon, and stars. Wainamoinen is born and spends years in the womb before emerging as an old, wise singer and enchanter who shapes the early world.
full_text: This legendary tale explores the depths of Finnish folklore. In primeval times, the Daughter of the Ether descends to the ocean. A duck nests on her knee, laying six golden eggs and one iron egg. These eggs break, forming the earth, sky, sun, moon, and stars. Wainamoinen is born and spends years in the womb before emerging as an old, wise singer and enchanter who shapes the early world. The narrative emphasizes the magical nature of the world and the eternal struggle between order and chaos, reflecting the spiritual beliefs of the ancient North.
moral: The balance of nature must be respected.

title: Blockhead Hans
summary: A story about a man who is considered a fool by society, but whose simple, honest nature and unwavering kindness lead to an unexpected positive outcome and the realization of his true worth.
full_text: In the rich tradition of Danish folklore, the tale of Blockhead Hans is told. A story about a man who is considered a fool by society, but whose simple, honest nature and unwavering kindness lead to an unexpected positive outcome and the realization of his true worth. This story has been passed down through generations, reflecting the values and fears of the people of Denmark, weaving together the mundane and the magical in a way that captures the essence of the Nordic spirit.
moral: Kindness is always rewarded in the end.

title: Bárðar Saga - The Guardian of Snæfell
summary: Bárðr, born of a human mother and a father who was half-giant and half-troll, settles in Snæfellsnes. After a series of family conflicts involving his brother and children, Bárðr gives away his land and vanishes into the Snæfellsjökull ice cap. He becomes the guardian spirit of the region, appearing in a grey cowl to help those in need during times of great difficulty.
full_text: In the ancient lands of Iceland, the story of Bárðar Saga - The Guardian of Snæfell has been told for generations. Bárðr, born of a human mother and a father who was half-giant and half-troll, settles in Snæfellsnes. After a series of family conflicts involving his brother and children, Bárðr gives away his land and vanishes into the Snæfellsjökull ice cap. He becomes the guardian spirit of the region, appearing in a grey cowl to help those in need during times of great difficulty. This legend serves as a reminder of the mysterious forces that once governed the north, where the boundary between the mundane and the supernatural was as thin as a winter's frost. The echoes of these events still linger in the wind and the stone, whispering to those who know how to listen.
moral: Respect the unseen forces of the world.

title: Bárðar Saga - The Mound of King Raknarr

With the help of a priest and a magic candle, Gestr enters Raknarr's burial mound and beheads the king and his 500 undead crew. However, the magic candle fails, and Gestr is only saved by the divine light of King Olaf, who drains the undead of their strength.

title: The Betrayal of Friends
summary: Two lifelong friends living in different parts of Greenland maintain a deep bond by exchanging gifts of seal and reindeer meat. However, jealousy grows in one friend, who decides to curse the other by rubbing seal fat with a piece of a corpse onto the food he shares. The betrayed friend, warned by his tornak (protective spirit), falls ill. The curse eventually consumes the betrayer, driving him to madness and a murderous hunger for his friend, ending in a tragic death in a cave.
full_text: In the fjords of the west and the open tundra of the east, two friends maintained a bond that defied the distances of Greenland. Each season, the western friend sent seal meat bundled in sealskin, and the eastern friend sent dried reindeer wrapped in birch bark. Their friendship was the envy of all who knew them until jealousy poisoned the eastern man's heart. He watched his western friend's reputation grow and felt his own name fade into the shadows. In his madness, he performed a dark ritual. He took a piece of seal fat and rubbed it against a corpse, whispering curses into the blubber. He packed it with his next gift and sent it westward. The western friend, a man of deep faith in the old spirits, was warned by his tornak, a protective spirit that guarded his family line. 'Do not eat the fat,' the tornak whispered in his dreams. 'Your brother has cursed you.' But it was too late. The curse had already entered his home. The man fell ill, his body wasting away. Meanwhile, the curse turned back on its creator. The eastern friend grew ravenous, driven by a murderous hunger for the man he had once loved. The villagers found his body frozen in a cave, his eyes wide open, a rictus of hunger on his face. The tornak had allowed the curse to consume the one who had unleashed it.
moral: Jealousy that festers into malice consumes the jealous before the envied.
