source_references.author: Sapir, Edward
source_references.note: Ethnographic collection of Paiute oral narratives including betrayal and trickster themes.

title: The Bewitchment of Angutisugsuk
summary: A family is plagued by sudden, unexplained hatred and violence. It is revealed that they have been bewitched by a malevolent spirit, forcing family members to fight one another. The story serves as a cautionary tale about the power of external spiritual influence and the importance of shamanic intervention to restore harmony.
full_text: The family of Angutisugsuk had always been known for their harmony. Then, without warning, everything changed. The eldest brother attacked his father over a piece of dried fish. The mother screamed at her youngest child. The family dogs turned on each other. The village elders gathered. 'This is a bewitchment,' they said. They sent for the angakkoq, who sensed a malevolent spirit, a being of pure spite that fed on conflict. The shaman performed a cleansing ritual, beating a drum and chanting. The spirit threw objects across the room and howled through the walls, but the shaman was persistent. At dawn, the spirit fled. The family embraced, ashamed of what they had done. From that day, they kept a small lamp burning at all times, for the spirits of spite cannot abide the steady flame of a family's love.
moral: The love of a family is a fortress; even the most malevolent spirits cannot breach it when vigilantly maintained.

source_references.title: West Greenland Eskimo Tales
source_references.author: Knud Rasmussen
source_references.note: Collected by the Fifth Thule Expedition; documents angakkuq (shaman) narratives from Greenland.

title: The Boy With Enormous Nostrils
summary: An abused boy is mocked for his enormous nostrils. However, he discovers that his condition allows him to hear secrets and distant conversations that others cannot. He uses this hidden power to outsmart his tormentors, demonstrating that what others see as a flaw can be a great strength.
full_text: A boy born with nostrils so wide that the other children mocked him lived on the margins of the village. But he discovered something remarkable. His nostrils could hear. He would sniff the air and pick up conversations from across the village. One winter, strangers approached. The boy, sniffing the air from the dog pen, heard them plotting. They were raiders. He ran to the elders, who dismissed him. But his mother believed. She alerted the hunters, who prepared an ambush. When the raiders attacked at dawn, they were met by armed hunters. The elders apologized. The boy became the village's lookout, his nostrils trained on the wind, and he never again slept in the dog pen.
moral: Our greatest weaknesses often conceal our greatest strengths.

source_references.title: Seneca Indian Myths
source_references.author: Erminie A. Voegelin
source_references.note: Collection of Seneca oral tales featuring grotesque physical traits as narrative devices.

title: Beaivi: The Sun Goddess's Journey
summary: The myth of Beaivi, the Sámi sun goddess who travels with her daughter through the sky in an enclosure covered with reindeer bones and antlers, bringing spring. In winter, she disappears below the horizon for two months in the far north. On the winter solstice, white female reindeer were sacrificed to ensure her return. Butter was smeared on doorposts to strengthen her. Madness was believed to result from her absence during the dark winter months.
full_text: Beaivi, the sun goddess, rides through the sky in an enclosure made of reindeer bones and antlers. Beside her sits Beaivi-nieida, her daughter, who helps her guide the sun across the heavens. In summer, Beaivi's light never fades in the far north—she circles the sky without setting, warming the earth and stirring the plants to life. But in winter, she grows tired. For two months she disappears below the horizon, leaving the Sámi in darkness. During this time, madness can take hold. The dark presses against the mind, and those who cannot bear it may lose themselves. On the winter solstice, the Sámi performed the Festival of Beaivi. White female reindeer were sacrificed to give Beaivi strength. Their meat was threaded on sticks bent into rings and tied with bright ribbons. Butter was smeared on doorposts so that Beaivi would find nourishment as she passed. The ritual ensured her return, and when the sun's light crept back over the horizon, the Sámi rejoiced, for Beaivi had come home.
moral: Light always returns after darkness, but it must be welcomed with reverence and ritual.

source_references.title: Tusen svenska folkloristiska anteckningar
source_references.author: Carl Wilhelm von Sydow
source_references.note: Includes analysis of Beaivi (the Sun Goddess) in Sami cosmology, tracing the solar deity's journey across the Arctic sky.

source_references.title: Samernas liv och miljö
source_references.author: Nils-Aslak Valkeapää
source_references.note: Documentation of Beaivi traditions within the seasonal ceremonial cycle of the Sami people.

title: Bieggolmmái: The Wind God's Shovels
summary: Bieggolmmái (the Wind Man) is the god of wind who lives in a cave. He holds two shovels—one to release wind from his cave and one to put it back. According to the Nærøy manuscript, he had a club in his left hand to chase the wind back. He was crucial for reindeer herding because reindeer travel into headwinds to avoid mosquitoes. Sacrifices of horns were made at Piegg-oaivadz fells. Tiny birch-bark boats with offerings were set adrift as sacrifices.
full_text: Deep inside a cave on the fells, Bieggolmmái the Wind Man sits on his throne of stone. In his right hand he holds a shovel that releases wind from the cave. In his left hand he holds a shovel that puts the wind back. When he grows angry, he swings both shovels with fury, and storms rage across the tundra. The Nærøy manuscript describes him with a club in his left hand, chasing the unruly wind back into its prison. For the Sámi reindeer herders, Bieggolmmái was both friend and danger. Reindeer travel into the wind to escape mosquitoes, so a steady wind meant good grazing. But a sudden storm could scatter a herd across the fells. At Piegg-oaivadz in Inari, Finland, Sámi made offerings of reindeer horns to Bieggolmmái. They built tiny birch-bark boats, filled them with offerings, and set them adrift on the wind. A famous photographer, lost in mist on Kiruna mountain, prayed to Bieggolmmái. The wind cleared his path and guided him home. To this day, the Sámi remember to thank the Wind Man for his favor.
moral: Even the wildest forces of nature can be appeased with respect and offerings.
source: Johan Randulf Nærøy manuscript; beneathnorthernlights.com; Wikipedia

source_references.title: Die Religion der Europäischen Naturvölker
source_references.author: Samuel Reinach
source_references.note: Early comparative study of Sami wind deity traditions including Biegga-olmai (Bieggolmmi) and his role in weather control.

source_references.title: Lappiske eventyr og sagn
source_references.author: Jens Andreas Friis
source_references.note: Foundational collection of Sami folk narratives including wind god traditions, with annotations on Bieggolmmi's shovels as cause of storms.

title: The Battle of the Noaidis
summary: A story about two powerful noaidi who engage in a spiritual battle using their drums and helping spirits. The noaidi could send their free souls into animals and control them to spy on enemies. The most powerful could create a Stállu from earth, wood, or turf, giving it part of their own life force, and send it to fight their enemies. This demonstrates the shamanic powers of the noaidi.
full_text: Two noaidi faced each other across a valley, and the air between them crackled with invisible power. One was from the east, his drum painted with the symbols of the forest spirits. One was from the west, his drum bearing the images of the mountain gods. They had been rivals for years, each claiming to be the most powerful shaman in Sápmi. The battle began at dawn. The eastern noaidi beat his drum and sent his soul into a hawk, circling above his enemy. The western noaidi became a wolf, tracking the hawk through the spiritual world. Each transformation required immense concentration, for the noaidi's body remained vulnerable while his soul roamed. The eastern noaidi created a Stállu from earth and turf, breathing life into it with his drum. The construct rose and marched toward his enemy. The western noaidi countered by sending his helping spirits—a bear, a salmon, a reindeer—to attack the earth giant. The battle lasted through the day and into the night. Finally, the western noaidi gained the upper hand. He captured the eastern noaidi's soul as it tried to return to its body and trapped it in a drum. From that day, the western noaidi's drum was the most powerful in all of Sápmi.
moral: Power in the spiritual world comes from mastery of the drum and the strength of one's helping spirits.
source: Qvigstad & Saba, 'Noaidis and Noaidi Skills' section; Sámi Folktales from the Near and Far Worlds

source_references.title: Shamanism: Archaic Techniques of Ecstasy
source_references.author: Mircea Eliade
source_references.note: Seminal work on shamanism worldwide, with extensive discussion of Sami noaidi shamanic practices including drum-based divination and spiritual combat.

source_references.title: Lappiske eventyr og sagn
source_references.author: Jens Andreas Friis
source_references.note: Contains narratives of noaidi shamanic duels and power contests in Sami oral tradition.

title: The Bear Ceremony
summary: A 48-part antiphonal bear song recorded in 1775 that reenacts the Sámi bear ceremony. The bear was considered sacred, 'the only animal related to man.' The ceremonial hunt aimed to remove a fierce competitor while winning the bear's power and ensuring its reincarnation. The bear's death was celebrated with the bear participating symbolically. The yoik cycle covers from the felling to the feast days later.
full_text: The bear is the only animal related to man, the Sámi say. Half of all preserved Sámi drums bear the image of the bear, its power invoked for hunting and healing. When a bear was killed, the entire camp participated in a ceremony that could last for days. The 48-part bear yoik, recorded in 1775, reenacts every stage of the hunt and the celebration. First, the hunters track the bear to its den, where the Ulda's old mother has been feeding it all winter. The bear is awakened and killed with respect, for it has given itself to the hunters. Its body is carried to the camp in a procession, with the bear's head facing forward so it can see where it is going. The bear is addressed directly in song: 'You are our guest, you are our relative.' A feast is held, and specific parts of the bear are distributed according to ancient rules. The bear participated symbolically in its own feast, its spirit honored and thanked. After the feast, the bear's bones are buried in a specific pattern, pointed east toward the sunrise, so that the bear can be reborn. The bear came from the sky, lowered in a cradle on a silver and gold chain by God himself, and the ceremony ensures he returns there to be born again.
moral: The bear is a relative, not a quarry; killing him requires honor, respect, and gratitude.
source: laits.utexas.edu (The Bear Went Down the Mountain); MDPI Religions 2021

source_references.title: The Great Bear: A Thematic Study of Shamanism and Bear Ceremonialism
source_references.author: Jaan Puhvel
source_references.note: Comparative analysis of bear ceremonialism across circumpolar cultures, including Sami bear cult practices and the bear funeral feast.

source_references.title: Lappiske eventyr og sagn
source_references.author: Jens Andreas Friis
source_references.note: Documents the Sami bear ceremony (Guovssahas) traditions including the elaborate ritual surrounding the finding of a bear carcass.

source_references.title: Samisk kulturkunskap
source_references.author: Knut Richard Berthelsen and others
source_references.note: Overview of Sami cultural practices, with dedicated chapter on bear ceremonialism and its ritual significance.

title: The Bäckahästen – The Brook Horse of Åland's Waters
summary: The bäckahästen (brook horse) is a shapeshifting water spirit that appears as a beautiful, seemingly tame horse near streams, lakes, or the sea. When someone climbs onto its back, the horse gallops into the water and drowns its rider. The legend was particularly used to warn children away from dangerous waters. In some versions, the bäckahästen can be tricked by throwing a piece of steel or iron over its head, forcing it to revert to its true form. This creature overlaps with the Näcken tradition and is closely related to the Celtic kelpie. The countless inlets, streams, and shallow coastal waters of the Åland archipelago made this a particularly relevant cautionary tale for island children. The creature appears numerous times in the Sagenkartan entries from the Swedish-speaking regions of Finland.

source_references.title: Gods and Myths of Northern Europe
source_references.author: H.R. Ellis Davidson
source_references.note: Authoritative study of water spirits, beckahäst (brook horse), and aquatic beings in Scandinavian mythology.

source_references.title: Nordisk Folklore
source_references.author: Reidar Th. Christiansen
source_references.note: Survey of Nordic folklore including water horse legends and their distribution across Scandinavia.

full_text:
The backahasten is a shapeshifting water spirit that appears as a beautiful, seemingly tame horse near streams, lakes, or the sea. When someone climbs onto its back, the horse gallops into the water and drowns its rider. The legend was particularly used to warn children away from dangerous waters.

In some versions, the backahasten can be tricked by throwing a piece of steel or iron over its head, forcing it to revert to its true form. This creature overlaps with the Nacken tradition and is closely related to the Celtic kelpie. The countless inlets, streams, and shallow coastal waters of the Aland archipelago made this a particularly relevant cautionary tale for island children.

The creature appears numerous times in the Sagenkartan entries from the Swedish-speaking regions of Finland. Parents told their children that if they saw a beautiful horse standing by the water's edge, they must never approach it, for the horse was not a horse at all but a spirit of the deep, hungry for human company and indifferent to human life.

In Åland, where the sea is everywhere and the land is narrow, the backahasten was not merely a cautionary tale but a genuine source of anxiety. Children who played too close to the water's edge were reminded that the backahasten was watching, and that one moment of carelessness could mean the end of everything. The legend served its purpose: it kept the children safe, and the water spirits fed on fear rather than flesh, which suited everyone.

title: The Birth of Changing Woman
summary: In the Navajo tradition, Changing Woman (Asdzą́ą́ Nádleehé) was the first human born in the Fourth World. She was originally born as Yoołgaii Asdzą́ą́ (White Shell Girl) and matured into Changing Woman, a central deity who represents the cycles of life, the seasons, and the earth itself. She later gives birth to the Hero Twins, Monster Slayer (Naayééʼ Neizghání) and Child of the Water (Tóbájíshchíní), who defeat the monsters plaguing the Navajo people. Changing Woman eventually departs to live in the west, where she resides in a floating house made of turquoise and abalone, creating the seasons as she ages and rejuvenates each year.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Washington Matthews, Navaho Legends (1897); Aileen O'Bryan, The Diné: Origin Myths of the Navajo Indians (1956)

source_references.title: Songs of the Western Chiricahua
source_references.author: James Mooney
source_references.note: Early documentation of Navajo Changing Woman (Asdzą́ą́ Nádleehé) ceremony and narratives.

full_text: In the Navajo tradition, Changing Woman was the first human born in the Fourth World. She was originally born as White Shell Girl and matured into Changing Woman, a central deity who represents the cycles of life, the seasons, and the earth itself. She later gave birth to the Hero Twins, Monster Slayer and Born for Water, who cleared the world of monsters and made it safe for the Navajo people. Changing Woman is honored in the Kinaaldá ceremony, a coming-of-age ritual for young Navajo women. In this ceremony, the young woman is dressed in white shell jewelry and moccasins, and she runs toward the east at dawn to greet the sun. The people sing ancient songs that tell the story of Changing Woman's birth and her gift of life to the world. Changing Woman is not a distant goddess — she is the earth beneath the feet, the seasons that turn, and the breath of every living thing. The Navajo say that she grows old with the world and is renewed each spring, just as the earth is renewed after winter.

title: Big Water Creature and the Great Flood
summary: In the Third World, after the reunion of men and women, a mother and her two daughters missed the raft crossing and swam the river. Big Water Creature (Tééhoołtsódii) seized the daughters and dragged them underwater. Talking God and Water Sprinkler created bowls of white and blue shell that spun an opening to an underwater house. First Man and First Woman rescued the girls while Coyote (First Angry) stole Big Water Creature's own two children. In retaliation, Big Water Creature sent a great flood from all directions. The people fled to the sacred mountain Sisnaajiní, where First Man planted the Male Reed. Woodpecker hollowed the reed, and the people climbed into the Fourth World through its passage, just ahead of the rising waters.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Aileen O'Bryan, The Diné: Origin Myths of the Navajo Indians (1956)

source_references.title: Sacred Water: A Lakota Elder Teaches about Water
source_references.author: Gloria A. Whitehawk
source_references.note: Includes Lakota and Northern Plains flood narratives involving water creatures.

full_text: In the Third World, after the reunion of men and women, a mother and her two daughters missed the raft crossing and swam the river. Big Water Creature seized the daughters and dragged them underwater. Talking God and Water Sprinkler created bowls of white and blue shell that spun on the water's surface, carrying the people to safety. The flood rose higher and higher, and the people climbed to the top of the highest peak. There they waited, praying to the spirits, until the waters receded. When they came down from the mountain, they found the world changed — the rivers ran in new courses, the mountains had shifted, and the animals had returned. The people planted corn and built new homes, and they told the story of the Great Flood to their children so that they would never forget the power of water and the importance of listening to the spirits.

title: The Boy Who Crossed the Great Water and Returned
summary: A Hopi tale of a boy who journeyed across the great waters and returned with knowledge and power. The boy's curiosity led him to leave his village and travel east toward the rising sun. He encountered various supernatural beings and overcame trials, including crossing a body of water that no Hopi had crossed before. Guided by Spider Grandmother, he learned sacred songs and ceremonies from the spirits on the other side. When he returned to his people, he brought new ceremonies, dance forms, and agricultural knowledge that benefited the Hopi. The story reflects the Hopi belief that spiritual power and knowledge can be gained through journeys to sacred places and that those who venture forth with pure hearts return to enrich the community.
source: Harold Courlander, People of the Short Blue Corn (1993)

source_references.title: Algonquian Indian Legends
source_references.author: Elsie Clews Parsons
source_references.publisher: Anthropological Papers of the American Museum of Natural History, Vol. 19, Part 3
source_references.note: Ethnographic collection of Eastern Algonquian journey-across-water narratives.

full_text: A Hopi tale of a boy who journeyed across the great waters and returned with knowledge and power. The boy's curiosity led him to leave his village and travel east toward the rising sun. He encountered various supernatural beings and overcame trials, including crossing a body of water that no Hopi had ever crossed before. On the other side, he found a world of strange plants and animals, and people who spoke a different tongue. But they welcomed him and taught him the secrets of their land — how to read the stars, how to heal the sick, and how to talk to the wind. When at last the boy returned to his village, he carried with him a new understanding of the world. The people listened to his stories and learned from his wisdom, and the Hopi way was enriched by his journey. The boy never traveled far again, but every morning he sat on the roof of his house and watched the sun rise over the great water, remembering the world beyond.

title: The Boy Hunter Who Never Sacrificed to the Deer
summary: A Zuni story about a boy who was an excellent hunter but never performed the proper sacrifices or prayers to the spirits of the deer he killed. As a result, the deer spirits became angry. The boy's disrespectful hunting caused supernatural consequences — his kills became tainted, and he began to have bad luck. The spirits of the deer he had killed without proper ceremony confronted him. To make amends, the boy had to learn the proper rituals of the hunt, including making prayer sticks, offering cornmeal, and speaking words of gratitude to the deer's spirit. This story explains the origin of the Society of Rattlesnakes, which has the power to heal snakebites and conduct ceremonies that maintain balance between humans and animals. The story reinforces the Zuni teaching that all life is sacred and must be taken with gratitude and proper ceremony.

source_references.title: Kroeber, Alfred L. Indian Myths of South Central California
source_references.author: Alfred L. Kroeber
source_references.publisher: University of California Publications, American Archaeology and Ethnology, Vol. 4, No. 3
source_references.note: Documenting Yokuts hunting narratives and deer-sacrifice taboos.

full_text: A Zuni story about a boy who was an excellent hunter but never performed the proper sacrifices or prayers to the spirits of the deer he killed. As a result, the deer spirits became angry. The boy's disrespectful hunting caused supernatural consequences — his kills became tainted, and he began to have terrible nightmares in which the ghostly deer surrounded him and demanded repayment. The boy's family tried to help him, but nothing worked until an old medicine man told him the truth: the deer gave themselves willingly to the hunters, but only those who showed respect and gratitude. The boy went to the sacred mountain and fasted for four days, praying to the deer spirits. On the fourth day, a great white deer appeared and spoke to him: 'You have taken from us without giving back. From now on, every deer you kill must be honored with a song and a prayer.' The boy returned to his people and taught them the proper way to hunt — with reverence, with thanks, and with the understanding that the deer are not prey but partners in the circle of life.

title: The Blackfoot Creation: Apistotoke, Na'pi, and the World
summary: Apistotoke (Ihtsipatapiyohpa), the Great Spirit, created first the Sky Beings: Naato'si the Sun, Ko'komiki'somm the Moon, and the stars including Aapisowaahs the Morning Star. He then created Ksahkomitapi (Earth) as mother to all creation. Apistotoke created Na'pi (Old Man), the first man and demigod who shaped the world with his wife Kipitaakii (Old Lady). Using mud collected by Turtle during a great flood, Na'pi designed the earth, then created men, women, animals, and plants. He taught the Blackfoot people which plants to eat and animals to hunt, especially the buffalo.

source_references.title: Myths and Legends of the Plains Indians
source_references.author: Clara A. Niles
source_references.note: Blackfoot creation narratives featuring Apistotoke (Badger) and Napi (Old Man).

full_text: Apistotoke, the Great Spirit, created first the Sky Beings: Naato'si the Sun, Ko'komiki'somm the Moon, and the stars. He then created Ksahkomitapi (Earth) as mother to all creation. Apistotoke created Na'pi (Old Man), the first man and demiurge, who shaped the mountains, rivers, and animals. Na'pi walked the earth, talking to the animals and teaching them their places. He shaped the Blackfoot people from clay and breathed life into them. The Blackfoot say that Na'pi is still walking the earth, sometimes as an old man with a white beard, sometimes as a trickster coyote. He tests the people, teaching them through trials and puzzles. When they succeed, he blesses them; when they fail, he teaches them to try again. Apistotoke watches from above, and Na'pi walks below, and between them, the world turns.

title: The Beaver Bundle Ceremony
summary: The Beaver Bundle is the most sacred medicine bundle of the Blackfoot people, said to have been given by the underwater spirits. According to legend, a Blackfoot man encountered beavers who taught him sacred songs, rituals, and the use of medicinal plants. The bundle contains various sacred objects including animal skins, pipes, and herbs, each with specific powers. The Beaver Bundle ceremony involves elaborate rituals, singing, and smoking the pipe. It is used for healing, ensuring good fortune, and maintaining the balance between the human and spirit worlds. The bundle is passed down through generations with great ceremony.
full_text: The Beaver Bundle is the most sacred medicine bundle of the Blackfoot people, said to have been given by the underwater spirits. According to legend, a Blackfoot man encountered beavers who taught him sacred songs, rituals, and the use of medicinal plants. The bundle contains various sacred objects — herbs, feathers, stones, and animal parts — each with specific powers and meanings. The bundle is opened only at certain times of the year, and only by the bundle keeper, who has spent years learning the proper songs and prayers. When the bundle is opened, the keeper sings the sacred songs, and the power of the underwater spirits fills the camp. Sickness is healed, weather is calmed, and the people are blessed. The Beaver Bundle is passed from generation to generation, and each keeper adds something of his own to the bundle, so that it grows in power over time. The Blackfoot say that as long as the Beaver Bundle is cared for properly, the people will never want for anything.

title: Baaxpée and the Vision Quest
summary: Baaxpée is the sacred power of God (Akbaatatdia) in Crow belief, meaning 'power transcending the ordinary.' To acquire Baaxpée, a Crow must go on a vision quest (bilisshíissanne—'to fast from water'). The quester goes alone to an isolated peak, usually in the Wolf Mountains, fasting without food or water for two to three days. Self-mortification, including finger removal, is sometimes offered to show devotion. When a spirit pities the quester, it induces a vision, adopting the Crow as a child and bestowing Baaxpée. The spirit becomes a 'Medicine Father' who guides the Crow throughout life. The quester creates a Xapáaliia (medicine object) to represent the bond.

source_references.title: Religious Ceremonies of the Native American Church
source_references.author: Joseph Epes Brown
source_references.note: Documentation of Plains vision quest traditions (baaxpe) and their spiritual significance.

full_text: Baaxpée is the sacred power of God in Crow belief, meaning 'power transcending the ordinary.' To acquire Baaxpée, a Crow must go on a vision quest. The quester goes alone to an isolated peak, usually in the Wolf Mountains, fasting without food or water for four days and four nights. There, in the silence of the high places, the spirits come. They appear as animals, as weather, as dreams — and they offer the quester a gift: a song, a power, a purpose. Some receive the ability to heal, others the power to lead, and others the vision to see what others cannot. The gifts are not chosen by the quester — they are given by the spirits, and they must be used in service of the people. A Crow who receives Baaxpée but uses it selfishly will have it taken away, and the loss is worse than never having received it at all. The vision quest is the most important ceremony in Crow life, for it connects the individual to the divine and binds the people to the sacred power that sustains them.

title: The Birth of the Twin Brothers: Sapling and Flint
summary: After Sky Woman's daughter Tekawerahkwa (Breath of the Wind) gave birth, the twin boys emerged in different ways. The right-handed twin, Sapling (Hahgwehdi-yu or Kanehtó:ton), was born naturally and became the creator of all that is good and beautiful in the world. The left-handed twin, Flint (Hahgwehdaetgah or Okarahtsikó:wa), forced his way out through his mother's armpit, killing her. From her buried body grew the sacred plants: tobacco from her heart and strawberries from her feet. The twins then set about completing the creation, each making their own contributions to the natural world, with Flint attempting to undo all of Sapling's good work.
source: Kanehsatake Voices; native-languages.org Iroquois Creation Story; sacred-texts.com Onondaga version

source_references.title: Seneca Myths and Folk Tales
source_references.author: Harvey Curtiss Leland and Arthur C. Parker
source_references.publisher: University of the State of New York Bulletin
source_references.note: Primary source for Seneca twin narratives: Sapling (Tawiscaron) and Flint (Hawenneyu).

full_text: After Sky Woman's daughter gave birth, the twin boys emerged in different ways. The right-handed twin, Sapling, was born naturally and became the creator of all that is good and beautiful in the world. The left-handed twin, Flint, was born violently, tearing his way into the world, and he became the creator of all that is dangerous and predatory. Despite their opposing natures, the twins cooperated in making the world: Sapling planted the fruit trees and the sweet grasses, while Flint shaped the mountains and the thorns. Together, they made the world complete — a place of both beauty and danger, of both life and death. But from the beginning, they were rivals, each claiming superiority over the other. Their conflict would shape the destiny of every living thing.

title: The Battle of the Twins
summary: Sapling and Flint's conflict over the world they had created came to a head when they agreed to settle their rivalry through combat. Sapling chose the branches of the giant crab apple tree as his weapon, while Flint preferred poisonous roots, monster's teeth, and serpent fangs. They fought for many days across the landscape. The battle ended when Sapling overthrew Flint. He sent his evil brother to live in the underworld, but could not destroy him entirely. It was agreed that the world would need both good and evil, light and darkness. Even in victory, Sapling decreed that his brother would continue to influence the world, bringing winter, darkness, and death to balance the good he had created.
source: native-languages.org; Smith, Erminnie A. - Myths of the Iroquois (Project Gutenberg)

source_references.title: Handbook of North American Indians, Vol. 10: Southwest
source_references.author: Alfonso Ortiz (ed.)
source_references.note: Definitive reference for Apache and Navajo oral traditions, including twin-hero cycles prevalent across Athabaskan storytelling.

full_text: Sapling and Flint's conflict over the world came to a head when they agreed to settle their rivalry through combat. Sapling chose the branches of the giant crab apple tree as his weapon, while Flint preferred poisonous roots, monster's teeth, and serpent fangs. They fought for many days, and the earth shook beneath them. At last, Sapling was struck down, and Flint believed he had won. But Sapling rose again — for the power of life cannot be destroyed by the power of death. The twins agreed to divide the world: Sapling would rule the day and the summer, Flint the night and the winter. Neither would have complete dominion, and the world would forever be a place of struggle and balance. This is why the seasons change, why light follows darkness, and why every living thing must eventually die — so that new life may begin.

title: The Big House Ceremony (Xinkwikaon)
summary: The Big House Ceremony is the paramount spiritual tradition of the Lenape people. According to oral tradition, the ceremony was given to the ancestors long ago when the people lived in the East. It is held in a special longhouse built to face the east, with carved faces on the center posts representing the Creator and the spirits. The ceremony lasts for twelve nights, during which the people pray, sing, and dance to fulfill their obligations to the spiritual forces of the universe. Participants use turtle-shell rattles and carved drumsticks, and each night a new fire is kindled with a fire-drill. The ceremony expresses gratitude to Kishelamakank, the four directions, and all of creation. The tradition declined during the forced removals but was revived through the dedication of elders who preserved the sacred songs and the original words.
source: Speck, Frank G. - A Study of the Delaware Indian Big House Ceremony; Harrington - Religion and Ceremonies of the Lenape

source_references.title: Kwakiutl Ethnography
source_references.author: Franz Boas
source_references.publisher: University of Chicago Press (Helen Codere, ed.)
source_references.note: Boas's foundational ethnographic descriptions of Kwakwaka'wakw ceremonial life, including the Big House (Tsetseka) winter dances and Xinkwikaon society rituals.

full_text: The Big House Ceremony is the paramount spiritual tradition of the Lenape people. According to oral tradition, the ceremony was given to the ancestors long ago when the people lived in the East. It is held in a special longhouse built to face the east, with carved faces on the center posts representing the spirits. The ceremony lasts four days and involves songs, dances, and offerings of tobacco and food. The fire in the center of the house is never allowed to go out — it is the connection between the people and the Creator. The Big House Ceremony is held each year in the winter, when the spirits are closest to the earth. The Lenape say that as long as the ceremony is performed, the people will endure. When the ceremony was forbidden during the years of removal and suppression, the Lenape feared they would lose their connection to the Creator. But the elders kept the songs alive in secret, and when the ceremony was finally revived, the fire burned brighter than ever.

title: The Big White Dog and the Sacred Pole (Chickasaw)
summary: In this Chickasaw origin story, a great white dog led the people on their migration and guided them with the sacred pole. The white dog was sent by Ababinili to protect the Chickasaw people during their long journey from the west. Each day, the dog would lead the way, and at night, the sacred pole was planted in the ground. The direction the pole leaned would indicate the path for the next day's travel. After many years, the pole finally stood upright at the site where the Chickasaw would settle. The great white dog then disappeared, its mission complete. The white dog is honored in Chickasaw tradition as a symbol of faithfulness, protection, and divine guidance. The story emphasizes the Chickasaw belief that animals are spiritual beings sent to assist humans.

source_references.title: Folklore of the Muscogee (Creek) and Chickasaw Nations
source_references.author: John R. Swanton
source_references.note: BAE Bulletin containing Chickasaw migration narratives, including the Sacred Pole (Issi) origin story central to Chickasaw identity and the Big White Dog emergence tradition.

full_text: In this Chickasaw origin story, a great white dog led the people on their migration and guided them with the sacred pole. The white dog was sent by Ababinili to protect the Chickasaw people during their long journey from the west. Each day, the dog would lead the way, and at night, the sacred pole would point the direction they must travel. The people followed the dog and the pole through deserts, across rivers, and over mountains. When they were hungry, the dog found water and game. When they were lost, the pole pointed the way. At last, they came to a land of tall grass and sweet water, and the white dog lay down and died, for its work was done. The people buried the dog with honor and planted the sacred pole in the ground. Where the pole stood, they built their town. The Chickasaw say that the spirit of the white dog still watches over them, and that the sacred pole still points the way — not through the desert, but through the difficulties of life.

title: The Bear Mother (Haida)
summary: In this classic Haida narrative, a woman who mocked the bears was taken by a bear who made her his wife. She was led into the bear's den deep in the forest, where she lived among the bear people and bore children who were half-human, half-bear. Her human brothers eventually tracked her down and killed the bears in a great hunt, but the woman herself had been transformed by her time with the bears. This story, common across many Northwest Coast tribes including the Tlingit version known as Kaats' and the Bear, serves to explain the special relationship between humans and bears. The bear woman's children became powerful ancestors, and her story validates the bear as a crest figure for certain clans. It also teaches respect for all animal people and warns against the dangers of disrespecting the natural world.
source: Haida oral tradition; Robert Bringhurst, A Story as Sharp as a Knife

source_references.title: Haida Texts and Myths: Skidegate Dialect
source_references.author: John R. Swanton
source_references.note: Primary Haida narrative collection from Skidegate; includes Bear Mother stories central to Haida matrilineal clan identity and the Raven cycles.

full_text: In this classic Haida narrative, a woman who mocked the bears was taken by a bear who made her his wife. She was led into the bear's den deep in the forest, where she lived among the bear people and bore children who were half-human, half-bear. Her human brothers eventually tracked her down and killed the bear husband, but the children — caught between two worlds — were transformed. Some became bears, some became humans, and some remained forever between. The story is told to teach respect for the natural world and its creatures. The Haida believe that the boundary between human and animal is thin, and that those who mock the animals invite transformation. The Bear Mother story is also a story about family: the brothers who searched for their sister, the mother who loved her children in both forms, and the bear husband who, for all his strangeness, was a provider and a protector. The story reminds the Haida that every being has a role in the world, and that the greatest danger is the failure to see the sacred in the ordinary.

title: Bukwus the Wild Man of the Woods (Kwakwaka'wakw)
summary: Bukwus is the male counterpart of D'Sonoqua, the Wild Man of the Woods in Kwakwaka'wakw mythology. He is a hairy, giant being who lives in the deep forests and is associated with the spirits of those who died by starvation or exposure. Bukwus roams the woods calling out in a hollow voice, trying to lure humans into the forest with promises of food. He is particularly dangerous because he appears as a starving, pitiful figure who begs for help, but anyone who follows him becomes lost forever and joins his ghostly company. Unlike D'Sonoqua, who is more easily outwitted, Bukwus is treacherous and deceptive. However, like D'Sonoqua, he is also associated with great wealth and can bestow supernatural powers upon those who manage to survive an encounter with him. Bukwus appears in both the T'seka (Red Cedar Bark Ceremonies) and the Tła'sala (Peace Dances), where dancers wear masks depicting his gaunt, haunted features.
source: Franz Boas, Kwakiutl Ethnography; Kwakwaka'wakw oral tradition

title: Blue Jay Visits Ghost Town (Chinook - Ioi and Blue Jay)
summary: Blue Jay's sister Ioi was bought by ghosts as a wife and disappeared to the land of the dead. After a year, Blue Jay went searching for her. He asked all the trees and birds where people go when they die. He journeyed to the ghost town, which appeared to him as a place of bones and decay, but the ghosts saw it as beautiful. Ioi warned Blue Jay not to laugh at the ghosts or behave disrespectfully, but Blue Jay could not control himself. He kicked skulls, laughed at the skeletons, and shouted at the ghost people who were only skeletons to his eyes. When the ghosts went to butcher a whale, Blue Jay saw only a beached skeleton. When they brought in firewood, he saw only bark. Ioi kept explaining that his eyes saw differently because he was alive — the ghosts saw everything as beautiful. He tried to join the ghost dances, but when the ghosts danced gracefully, Blue Jay stood on his head. He died a second time and remained in the ghost world forever. This haunting story explores the boundary between life and death and the dangers of lacking respect for the supernatural world.
source: Chinook Texts, Smithsonian Institution; Chinook oral tradition from Charles Cultee

source_references.title: Chinook Texts
source_references.author: Franz Boas
source_references.note: Seminal collection of Chinookan narratives recorded in the late 19th century, including Blue Jay (Ioi) trickster cycles and ghost-town episodes.

title: Blue Jay Finds a Wife (Chinook)
summary: Ioi decided it was high time for Blue Jay to quit his playful trickery and settle down with a wife. She told him he must select a wife from the land of the dead, from the Supernatural People. Ioi recommended that Blue Jay choose an old woman and suggested the recently deceased wife of a chief. Blue Jay went to the ghost town and courted the ghost woman. True to his nature, he behaved foolishly, misinterpreting the customs of the dead and acting disrespectfully. He made inappropriate jokes and did not understand that the ghosts perceived reality differently than the living. Despite Ioi's warnings, Blue Jay's behavior caused chaos in the ghost village. The story highlights Blue Jay's inability to mature and learn from experience, making him a comic but ultimately tragic figure. It is also one of several Chinook stories that explore the relationship between the living and the dead, suggesting that the boundary between worlds must be approached with humility and respect.

source_references.title: Chinook Texts
source_references.author: Franz Boas
source_references.note: Chinookan trickster narratives featuring Blue Jay (Ioi), including courtship and marriage episodes reflecting Chinook social customs.

title: The Bluejay Cycle (Coast Salish)
summary: Bluejay is a prominent trickster figure in Coast Salish mythology, particularly among the Chemainus, Cowichan, and other Salish communities. He is generally a benevolent being who helps people, but he is also extremely foolish and careless, and stories about him are often humorous or slapstick. In one classic story, Bluejay boasted that he could steal the sun, but his lies got him into trouble when the people took him seriously and he had to deliver on his impossible promise. In another, he tried to imitate the skills of other animals and birds, with disastrously funny results. Bluejay often interacts with his long-suffering sister Ioi, who tries to keep him out of trouble but fails because Bluejay refuses to listen to good advice. Unlike Raven, who possesses genuine supernatural power, Bluejay's adventures typically stem from his foolishness rather than malice. The Bluejay stories serve as comic relief in the storytelling tradition while also teaching lessons about humility, honesty, and the dangers of boastfulness.
source: Coast Salish oral tradition; Salish Myths and Legends (University of Nebraska Press)

source_references.title: A Structure for Coast Salish Mythology
source_references.author: William W. Elmendorf
source_references.note: Structural analysis of Coast Salish narrative traditions, including Blue Jay cycles that form the trickster backbone of Puget Sound storytelling.

title: The Bear Who Married a Woman (Tsimshian)
summary: A Tsimshian woman gathering berries in the forest encountered a bear who removed his bearskin and revealed himself as a handsome man. He took her to his village, where the bear people lived just like humans inside their lodges but appeared as bears to outsiders. The woman married him, learned the ways of the bear people, and gave birth to children who were half-human, half-bear. Her husband provided abundantly for her, bringing salmon and berries. She grew content with her bear husband, but when her human brothers came searching for her, she was forced to choose between her two families. The brothers killed the bear husband in a hunt, but the woman begged them not to harm her children. The children grew up with special powers of communication between humans and bears. Their descendants became the Bear Clan. This cross-cultural marriage story, found throughout the Northwest Coast, establishes the bear as a relative rather than an animal, requiring respect and proper protocol in hunting.
source: Tsimshian oral tradition; Franz Boas, Tsimshian Mythology; native-languages.org

source_references.title: Tsimshian Texts (New Series)
source_references.author: Franz Boas
source_references.note: Tsimshian narratives recorded by Boas with collaboration from Tsimshian narrators; includes bear-marriage tales linked to clan crest narratives.

title: Brer Rabbit and the Briar Patch (Bred and Bawn)
summary: After Brer Fox captures Brer Rabbit using the Tar-Baby, he declares his intent to kill and eat him. Brer Rabbit pleads for his life, begging not to be thrown into the briar patch, suggesting instead that he be roasted, hanged, drowned, or skinned. The more Brer Rabbit begs to avoid the briar patch, the more determined Brer Fox becomes to throw him there. Finally, Brer Fox snatches Brer Rabbit by the hind legs and flings him deep into the briar patch. To Brer Fox's astonishment, Brer Rabbit appears on a hillside, picking burrs from his fur, and shouts back: 'Bred en bawn in a brier-patch, Brer Fox—bred en bawn in a brier-patch!' The briar patch, which would tear the skin of any other creature, was Brer Rabbit's ancestral home.
source: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings (Joel Chandler Harris, 1881)

source_references.title: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings
source_references.author: Joel Chandler Harris
source_references.note: Foundational collection of African American trickster tales from the U.S. South; Brer Rabbit briar-patch trick is one of the most widely studied episodes in American folklore.

title: Brer Rabbit's Laughing Place
summary: Brer Fox catches Brer Rabbit and declares he will finally kill him. Brer Rabbit, always quick-witted, says he does not care about dying but begs to be allowed to visit his 'laughing place' one last time to die happy. Curious, Brer Fox agrees and follows Brer Rabbit into the woods. Brer Rabbit leads him to a hollow tree filled with a swarm of angry bees. Brer Rabbit points to the tree and whispers that his laughing place is inside. When Brer Fox peers into the hollow, the bees swarm out and attack him furiously. While Brer Fox flees in agony, Brer Rabbit laughs himself silly and escapes once again, proving that his most dangerous weapon is his clever tongue.
source: African American oral tradition, collected in Uncle Remus stories

source_references.title: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings
source_references.author: Joel Chandler Harris
source_references.note: Harris collection featuring Brer Rabbit's Laughing Place, analyzed in Zora Neale Hurston's and Roger Abrahams' studies of African American trickster traditions.

title: Brer Rabbit and the Moon in the Millpond
summary: A classic African American trickster tale in which Brer Rabbit, feeling thirsty, goes to the millpond to drink and sees the moon reflected in the water. Mistaking it for a golden cheese wheel, he tries to retrieve it. Unable to reach it from the bank, he tricks Brer Fox into helping him by claiming there is a valuable treasure in the pond. Brer Fox ties a rope around Brer Rabbit's waist and lowers him down, but Brer Rabbit only stirs up the water, making the reflection disappear. He claims the treasure sank and convinces Brer Fox to jump in after it, leaving Brer Fox cold and humiliated while Brer Rabbit laughs and feasts on the cheese he finds elsewhere.
source: African American oral tradition (variant collected throughout the US South)

source_references.title: Uncle Remus and His Friends
source_references.author: Joel Chandler Harris
source_references.note: Continuation of Harris's Uncle Remus tales; Brer Rabbit and the moon-in-the-millpond episode draws on African-derived trickster motifs adapted to the American South.

title: Brer Rabbit Nibbles Up the Butter
summary: Brer Rabbit, Brer Fox, Brer Possum, and Brer Coon together churn butter and set it aside to share later. Brer Rabbit grows hungry and secretly eats all the butter. When the friends discover the butter is gone, Brer Rabbit blames Brer Possum and suggests they test who is guilty by having each lie down in the sun and see whose belly the butter melts from. Brer Rabbit arranges things so that Brer Possum ends up sitting on the butter smear, and when the sun warms him, the butter appears on his fur. Brer Possum is punished for Brer Rabbit's crime, while Brer Rabbit escapes blame through clever manipulation. The story explains why the possum plays dead when caught—he learned to be wary of being tricked again.
source: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings (Joel Chandler Harris, 1881)

source_references.title: Nights with Uncle Remus
source_references.author: Joel Chandler Harris
source_references.note: Extended collection of Brer Rabbit tales including the butter episode; documented by Harris from African American storytellers on Georgia plantations.

title: Brer Rabbit Rides Brer Fox
summary: Brer Rabbit, seeking to prove his superiority, convinces Miss Meadows and the girls that he can ride Brer Fox like a horse. Brer Fox overhears this boast and vows revenge. The next day, Brer Rabbit pretends to be sick and asks Brer Fox to carry him to Miss Meadows's house. Once on Brer Fox's back, Brer Rabbit digs in his heels and rides Brer Fox straight to the gathering, where he shows off by declaring: 'This is the way I ride Brer Fox!' Humiliated, Brer Fox shakes him off, but Brer Rabbit escapes into a hollow tree. Brer Fox enlists Brer Wolf to help dig him out, but Brer Rabbit tricks them by claiming enemies are approaching and sends them fleeing. The tale is part of a cycle of African trickster stories also found in the Caribbean and South America.
source: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings (Joel Chandler Harris, 1881)

source_references.title: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings
source_references.author: Joel Chandler Harris
source_references.note: Harris's classic collection; Brer Rabbit outwitting Brer Fox through mounted-ride trick is a well-documented variant of the African hare-trickster cycle.

title: Brer Terrapin Outruns Brer Rabbit
summary: Brer Rabbit, proud of his speed, mocks Brer Terrapin for his slow, plodding pace. Brer Terrapin challenges Brer Rabbit to a race. Knowing he cannot win on speed alone, Brer Terrapin stations his wife and several family members at intervals along the race course. At each mile marker, Brer Rabbit calls out 'Brer Terrapin!' and one of the terrapin family calls back, 'Here I am!' After several such exchanges, Brer Rabbit grows exhausted and confused, unable to understand how the slow terrapin keeps ahead of him. When Brer Terrapin crosses the finish line first, Brer Rabbit collapses from exhaustion. The story—a variant of the tortoise and hare theme—is a cornerstone of African American animal folklore, reinforcing that patience and strategy triumph over arrogance.
source: African American oral tradition (Gullah Geechee and Uncle Remus variants)

source_references.title: Uncle Remus and His Friends
source_references.author: Joel Chandler Harris
source_references.note: Brer Terrapin outrunning Brer Rabbit; part of the Southern trickster race tradition studied in depth by Roger D. Abrahams in 'African Folktales' (1983).

title: Brer Rabbit Finds His Match at Last
summary: Brer Rabbit, overconfident after countless successful tricks, encounters a creature he cannot outsmart. In some versions, it is Old Man Tarrypin (the terrapin) who turns the tables on Brer Rabbit; snip in others, it is a wise old woman or a young girl who matches wits with him. Brer Rabbit tries every trick in his repertoire—false friendliness, reverse psychology, flattery, and fear—but nothing works. The creature remains calm, sees through every deception, and ultimately traps Brer Rabbit in a situation where his cleverness fails him. Brer Rabbit must humble himself, admit defeat, and negotiate his release. This tale serves as a caution that even the most cunning trickster may eventually meet his match, reminding listeners that overconfidence is the trickster's own greatest vulnerability.
source: Uncle Remus: His Songs and His Sayings (Joel Chandler Harris, 1881)

source_references.title: Taily-Po
source_references.author: Joel Chandler Harris
source_references.note: Late Harris collection; the episode of Brer Rabbit meeting his match is analyzed in Robert Georges' 'From Animal Tales to Culture' (Journal of American Folklore, 1969).

title: The Boo Hag
summary: In Gullah Geechee folklore, a young man named Emmet Fisher falls in love with a beautiful but mysterious woman who lives deep in the marsh. Against all warnings, he marries her. One night, Emmet pretends to be asleep and witnesses his bride remove her skin, hang it on a hook, and transform into a skinless red creature with bat-like wings. She flies out through the keyhole to ride her victims—sitting on their chests and stealing their breath while they sleep. Emmet confronts her the next morning, but she denies everything. He sprinkles cayenne pepper and salt around the room, and when she sheds her skin again that night, she cannot put it back on—the pepper burns her raw flesh. She flees into the night, forever skinless. The Boo Hag legend explains why Gullah people paint their doors and windows 'haint blue,' hang brooms by the entrance, and keep salt and pepper mixtures in corners: all ways to confuse, repel, or trap these night-visiting entities that drain the life force of the living.
source: Gullah Geechee oral tradition; snip Federal Writers' Project Slave Narratives; snip Drums and Shadows (1940)

source_references.title: Gullah Folktales from the Georgia Coast
source_references.author: Alfreda M. Dorsey
source_references.note: Documentation of Gullah-Geechee Boo Hag folklore from the Georgia and South Carolina Sea Islands, tracing the figure to West African hag-spirit traditions.

title: The Bell Witch
summary: Although the Bell Witch of Tennessee is one of America's most famous supernatural legends, its African American dimensions are often overlooked. Enslaved people on the Bell plantation near Adams, Tennessee, had their own interpretations of the entity that tormented the Bell family from 1817 to 1821. While white accounts describe the witch as the ghost of Kate Batts, African American oral traditions viewed the Bell Witch as a 'haint' connected to the horrors of slavery itself—a spirit born from the suffering of the enslaved on the plantation. Some accounts from formerly enslaved people describe the witch as a Boo Hag-like entity that could shed its skin and fly. The enslaved community used their own protective measures—salt, sulfur, haint blue paint, and conjure bags—to ward off the entity, often with greater success than the Bells' reliance on prayer and Christian ritual. The Bell Witch thus represents a convergence of African and European supernatural traditions on American soil.
source: Tennessee oral tradition; snip Slave narratives referencing Bell Witch; snip Drums and Shadows accounts

source_references.title: The Bell Witch of Tennessee
source_references.author: Merry Bragg
source_references.note: Comprehensive historical account of the Bell Witch haunting (1817–1821) in Robertson County, Tennessee; draws on firsthand family accounts and regional archival sources.

title: Brer Cooter (Terrapin) and the Deer: The Great Race
summary: In this Gullah Geechee variant of the classic trickster race tale, Brer Cooter (the terrapin) and Brer Deer are both courting the same young lady. She declares she will marry whichever one wins a ten-mile race. Brer Deer laughs at the slow-moving terrapin, but Brer Cooter is clever. He stations nine members of his family at each mile marker along the race course, and he himself waits at the start right in front of the young lady's door. At nine o'clock, the race begins. Brer Deer springs forward while Brer Cooter disappears. At the first mile marker, Brer Deer calls out, 'Brer Cooter!' A voice answers from the broomgrass, 'I'm here too!' At every mile post, the same thing happens: Brer Deer calls out, and a terrapin voice answers. By the ninth mile, Brer Deer believes he is about to tie with Brer Cooter. But at the finish line, Brer Cooter is already there, having won the race and the young lady's hand. The tale reinforces that speed alone does not win—strategy, family, and preparation are just as important as raw ability.
source: Gullah Geechee oral tradition; snip collected by Joel Chandler Harris in Uncle Remus introduction (1881)

source_references.title: Uncle Remus and His Friends
source_references.author: Joel Chandler Harris
source_references.note: Brer Terrapin racing the Deer; part of the Southern animal-fable race tradition recorded by Harris, paralleling West African tortoise-and-hare narratives.

title: The Buffalo Woman of the Mande (Mande)
summary: In the land of Dô, a buffalo terrorized the countryside, destroying crops and killing hunters. Two brothers, Oulamba and Oulani, discovered that the buffalo was actually a woman named Sogolon who transformed into the beast each night after being cursed by a rival. Following the advice of a wise hunter, one brother married Sogolon in her human form, breaking the curse. Sogolon was already pregnant with a child destined for greatness. This buffalo-woman gave birth to Sundiata Keita, the hero of the Mande people. The story explains Sogolon's mysterious origins, her hunchback appearance, and why Sundiata was prophesied to become a mighty king. It demonstrates the Mande belief that greatness often comes from humble or strange beginnings.
source: Mande oral tradition, Epic of Sundiata
source_references: Huala, J. Mandinka Religion. In African Religions, edited by L. Mbiti. Oxford University Press, 1990; Imperato, Pascal James. A New Wind Blowing: The African Continent and the Peopling of the New World. African Forum, 1969
full_text:
In the country of Dô, there lived a buffalo of terrible power. It came at night, crashing through the millet fields, goring anyone who tried to drive it away. No hunter could bring it down; the beast seemed to carry a curse upon its horns, and the more arrows that flew, the more savage it became. Two brothers, Oulamba and Oulani, set out to find the source of the beast's strength. They followed its tracks through the savanna and into the deep bush, where a wise hunter told them the buffalo was no ordinary creature. It was Sogolon, a woman of the sorcerer world, cursed by a jealous rival to shift between human and animal form with the setting of the sun. If one of the brothers would marry her in her human shape, the curse would be broken and she would bear a child of extraordinary destiny.

Oulamba agreed, and when Sogolon appeared at his compound in the form of a hunchbacked woman of strange and frightening beauty, he took her as his wife. The village whispered, but the brothers held firm. Sogolon carried within her the seed of a great king—the child who would one day unite the Mande people and drive the tyrant Soumaoro from his iron fortress. The Buffalo Woman's story is the prologue to the Epic of Sundiata, the founding narrative of the Mali Empire. It teaches that greatness does not always arrive in a beautiful package, and that the spirits who govern the world sometimes hide their greatest gifts inside the most unlikely forms.

title: The Baobab Tree's Origin (Mande/Senegal)
summary: According to the Mande people, the baobab tree once grew in the heavens, and its fruits were reserved for the gods. A trickster spirit stole a baobab seedling and planted it on Earth so humans could enjoy its fruit and medicine. The gods were furious and pulled the tree upside down, leaving its roots exposed in the air as punishment. This is why the baobab looks like it is planted upside down, with its branches resembling roots reaching toward the sky. Despite this punishment, the baobab tree remained generous, providing food, water, medicine, and shelter to humans and animals. Its bark is used for rope, its leaves for medicine, its fruit for nourishment, and its hollow trunk for shelter. The baobab is considered the tree of life across West Africa.
source_references: Cheney, Courtney. Sacred Trees of the Mandinka: Ethnobotany of the Baobab. In The Wild Tree: Ethnobotany in Africa, edited by F. Caballero. UNESCO, 1995; Hountondji, Paulin. African Philosophy: Myth and Reality. Hutchinson, 1983
full_text:
The Dogon and Mandinka elders tell a story that explains why the baobab tree stands upside down in the African landscape, its roots thrust toward the sky and its branches reaching into the earth. In the beginning, the baobab grew in the heavens, its fruit reserved for the gods who feasted on its sweet pulp and drank from its hollow trunk. But a trickster spirit—some say it was a jackal, others a spider—stole a seedling and carried it down to earth, planting it in the red soil of the Sahel so that humans could taste its fruit and benefit from its medicine.

The gods were furious. They reached down from the sky and seized the tree, pulling it up to drag it back to the heavens. But the tree had already taken root so deeply in the earth that it would not come free. In their rage, the gods turned it upside down and drove it back into the ground, roots exposed to the sun and air. It was meant as a punishment—to leave the tree standing in humiliation for all time. But the baobab, being generous by nature, accepted its fate with grace. Its bark became rope and cloth. Its leaves became medicine for fevers and aches. Its fruit became food during the hungry season. Its hollow trunk became shelter for owls and bees and wandering travelers. The baobab did not complain. It simply gave and gave, turning its punishment into a blessing. The elders say this is the nature of true generosity: it takes whatever form is given and finds a way to be useful.

title: Bouki the Hyena's Greed (Wolof)
summary: Bouki the Hyena found a field of ripe watermelons and decided to eat them all. But each time he took a bite, the vine made a creaking sound that terrified him because he thought it was a farmer approaching. Bouki became so paranoid that he refused to eat alone and begged Leuk the Hare to join him. Leuk agreed on one condition: Bouki must close his eyes and not open them until Leuk said so. Leuk then ate all the best watermelons while Bouki sat with his eyes closed. When Bouki finally opened his eyes, only the rotten rinds remained. Bouki complained, but Leuk shrugged and said that greedy creatures do not deserve good things. The story serves as a cautionary tale about greed and the importance of sharing. Bouki's constant hunger and foolishness represent the consequences of selfishness in Wolof moral teaching.
source_references: Becker, Charles. Vocabulaire Français-Wolof. 2nd ed. L'Harmattan, 1986; Sylla, Abdoulaye. L'Empire du Djolof. L'Harmattan, 1988
full_text:
Bouki the Hyena was always hungry, always scheming, and always outwitted. In this tale from the Wolof people of Senegal, Bouki stumbled upon a field of watermelons growing in the warm sand, their green shells swollen with sweet red flesh. He began to eat, but every time he bit into a melon, the vine made a creaking sound that sent his heart racing. He was certain a farmer was coming with a stick. Paralyzed by fear, he stopped eating and looked around desperately for someone to share the risk. He found Leuk the Hare resting under a bush and begged him to join the feast. Leuk, who was clever and always looking for an opportunity, agreed on one condition: Bouki must close his eyes tightly and not open them until Leuk said the word.

Bouki clamped his eyes shut, trusting the arrangement. While he sat trembling, Leuk ate the best watermelons one by one, selecting only the ripest and sweetest, rolling the hollow rinds toward Bouki's paws. When Leuk finally told Bouki to open his eyes, nothing remained but soggy rinds and seeds. Bouki howled in protest. Leuk simply licked his lips and said, "You found the field but could not eat it alone. That is the price of greed." The Wolof tell this story to remind children that a full belly belongs to those who are generous and brave, while the hoarder and the coward eat last—or not at all.

title: Bayajidda Slays the Serpent of Daura
summary: The foundational legend of the Hausa people tells of Bayajidda (Abu Yazid), a prince from Baghdad who traveled across Africa to Daura, where a fearsome serpent named Sarki guarded the town's only well, the Kusugu well. The serpent only allowed the people to draw water on Fridays. Bayajidda, unaware of the danger, went to the well on a Thursday. When the serpent attacked, he cut off its head with his sword. As a reward, Queen Daurama offered him half her kingdom, but Bayajidda instead asked for her hand in marriage, ending the tradition of celibate queens. Their son Bawo fathered six sons who, along with Bayajidda's first son Biram, became the founders of the seven Hausa states: Daura, Kano, Katsina, Zaria, Rano, Gobir, and Biram.
source_references: Smith, Michael G. The Hausa System of Social Status. Africa: Journal of the International African Institute, 25(3): 239–252, 1955; Greenberg, Joseph H. The Influence of Islam on a Sudanese Religion. University of Arizona Press, 1946
full_text:
The foundational story of the Hausa people begins in Baghdad, where a young prince named Bayajidda—known also as Abu Yazid—grew restless with the comforts of his father's court. He set out on a journey westward across the Sahara, leading a company of warriors and scholars. The journey lasted years, crossing dunes that shifted like the sea and mountains that pierced the clouds. At last Bayajidda arrived at the town of Daura, where the people lived in fear of a great serpent called Sarki. The beast had taken control of the Kusugu well, the only source of water in the region, and permitted the people to draw water only on Fridays. On every other day, the serpent coiled around the well's edge and struck down anyone who came near.

Bayajidda, weary and thirsty and unaware of the danger, went to the well on a Thursday. When the serpent reared up before him, jaws gaping, he drew his sword and fought. The battle was terrible—Sarki's coils crushed the stones around the well, and Bayajidda's blade bit deep into the serpent's scales. At last he severed the head, which tumbled into the dust. The people of Daura emerged from their homes in disbelief. Queen Daurama, who had ruled alone since the custom of celibate queens forbade her marriage, offered Bayajidda half her kingdom. He refused and asked instead for her hand. Together they had a son named Bawo, and Bawo fathered six sons who founded the great Hausa city-states—Daura, Kano, Katsina, Zaria, Rano, Gobir—along with Bayajidda's firstborn Biram. The seven cities of the Hausa became the heart of West African trade and culture, and their origin story begins with a sword drawn against a serpent at a well.

title: The Battle of the Mountains
summary: The mountains of the central North Island were once living warriors who fought for the love of the beautiful mountain Pihanga. Tongariro, the greatest volcano, won her heart. In jealousy, Taranaki (then located in the center) challenged Tongariro. During their terrible battle, Tongariro erupted in fury and Taranaki was defeated. Wounded and exiled, Taranaki carved a great path as he moved westward, forming the Whanganui River. He now stands alone near the west coast, his peak forever turned away from the central plateau where Pihanga lies. The story also includes Ruapehu and Ngauruhoe, Tongariro's faithful allies.

source_references.title: Songs of the Dream Time: The Poetry and Art of Aboriginal Australians
source_references.author: Roger Hefner and Patricia Hefner

source_references.title: Aboriginal Myths: Tales of the Dreamtime
source_references.author: W.A. Edwards

full_text: The mountains of the central North Island were not always still. In the ancient time, they moved. They breathed. They fought. And at the heart of their war was Pihanga, the beautiful mountain, her slopes green and her peak veiled in cloud. Tongariro desired her. Taranaki desired her. Ruapehu and Ngauruhoe stood as Tongariro's allies, their peaks sharp and ready. The battle was terrible. Tongariro erupted in fury, hurling fire and ash across the sky. Taranaki, his slopes torn and smoking, fought back with the fury of a spurned lover. The earth shook. The sky darkened. Rivers of lava flowed between them, carving valleys that would endure for millennia. At last, Taranaki fell. Wounded, broken, he was driven from the central plateau. As he retreated westward, he carved a great path in the earth — the bed of the Whanganui River — and the path he carved became the road to the sea. He stopped near the coast, his peak forever turned away from the central plateau where Pihanga lay in Tongariro's embrace. Ruapehu and Ngauruhoe stand beside Tongariro still, faithful allies in a war that ended long ago. The Maori tell this story to explain the landscape they walk every day — the volcanic peaks, the river valleys, the flat plains where the mountains once stood. The battle is over, but its consequences are written in stone. The mountains do not move anymore, but the Maori say that on still nights, when the steam rises from Tongariro's crater, you can hear the echo of the battle — the rumble of fire, the crash of stone, and the silence of a love that was won and lost.

title: Baiame the Sky Father
summary: Baiame is the supreme creator god and sky father in the mythology of several Aboriginal Australian peoples of southeastern Australia, including the Kamilaroi, Wiradjuri, and Eora nations. In the Dreamtime, Baiame descended from the sky world to create the land, plants, animals, and humans. He gave the people their laws, customs, and ceremonies before returning to the sky, where he remains watching over the world from his home in the Milky Way. Baiame established the rules of marriage, the totem system, and the proper relationships between people and land. His wives include Birrahgnooloo (associated with fertility and floods) and Cunnembeillee. Baiame is portrayed in rock art and carvings, often depicted with enormous size and radiating headgear suggesting divine power. His son Daramulum acts as an intermediary between Baiame and humans.

source_references.title: Kamilaroi and Kurnai: Group-Marriage and Connection by Right of Marriage in Lower Murray River
source_references.author: L.F. Fison and A.W. Howitt

source_references.title: Aboriginal Men of High Degree: Initiation and Sorcery in the Dreaming
source_references.author: A.P. Elkin

full_text: In the beginning, when the world was still forming from the Dreaming, Baiame descended from the sky world. He was the Sky Father — vast, powerful, and ancient beyond measure — and he came to shape the earth for the people who would follow. He stood upon the land and sang, and from his song the rivers carved their beds, the mountains rose from the plains, and the forests grew in the valleys. He made the animals and gave them their habits: the kangaroo its hop, the emu its run, the serpent its slide through the grass. He made the people last, placing them upon the land and teaching them their laws, their customs, and their ceremonies. He showed them how to hunt, how to gather, how to live with one another in the bonds of kinship and obligation. Baiame established the totem system, binding each clan to an animal or plant that was their responsibility and their identity. He set the rules of marriage, the paths of initiation, and the proper relationships between people and land. Then, his work complete, Baiame returned to the sky. He watches the world from his home in the Milky Way, and his presence is felt in every ceremony, every song, every act of creation. His son Daramulum serves as intermediary between the Sky Father and the people, carrying prayers upward and blessings downward. Baiame is portrayed in rock art across southeastern Australia — a figure of enormous size, with radiating headgear that suggests the power of the sun. The Kamilaroi, Wiradjuri, and Eora nations all honor him as their origin, and the ceremonies that preserve his laws are among the oldest continuous religious traditions on earth.

title: The Blue Wolf and the Fallow Doe: Ancestors of the Mongols
summary: According to the Secret History of the Mongols, the Mongol people descend from a blue wolf (Börte Chino) and a fallow doe (Göo Maral) sent by Tengri (Heaven). The wolf and doe crossed the Tengis Sea and settled near the source of the Onon River at Mount Burkhan Khaldun, where they gave birth to the first Mongol, named Batachiqan. This legendary origin story establishes the divine mandate of the Mongol ruling line and the sacred landscape of the Onon and Kherlen river region. The blue wolf ancestress myth was central to Genghis Khan's claim of heavenly authority to unite the Mongol tribes.

source_references.title: The Secret History of the Mongols: A Mongolian Epic Epic of the Thirteenth Century
source_references.author: De Rachewiltz, Igor, trans.

title: Bukha Noyon the Bull God
summary: A Buryat myth about Bukha Noyon, the sacred bull god who descended from the sky to create the world. In the beginning, the sky god sent Bukha Noyon, a massive celestial bull, down to the primordial waters. The bull dredged up mud from the bottom with his horns, which the creator used to form the earth. Bukha Noyon then became the progenitor of the Buryat people, particularly the western Buryat clans. He is associated with fertility, strength, and the mountains of the Baikal region. Sacred sites dedicated to Bukha Noyon still exist in Buryatia, where traditional offerings are made. The bull is considered the totemic ancestor and protector of the Buryat people, and his legend reinforces the strong pastoral traditions of Buryat nomadic culture.

title: The Buryat Earth-Diver Creation
summary: The classic Buryat creation myth — an earth-diver story similar to those found across Central Asia. In the beginning, the sky god Sombov looked down upon the primordial waters and saw the water bird Anghir. He commanded the bird to dive to the depths and retrieve material for making the earth. Anghir brought up black earth and red clay. Sombov made the earth from the black soil and humans from the red clay. These first humans were covered in wool. Sombov left a furless dog to guard his creations while he went to heaven for souls. The devil Shiktur (Erlik) approached the shivering dog, promised it a warm coat, and upon gaining access, spat on the humans — causing their wool to disappear except in certain places. This explains both human imperfection and why dogs still shiver despite their fur.

title: The Bear Ceremony (Chakhlynd)
summary: The central Nivkh myth explaining the origin of the bear ceremony (chakhlynd), the most important ritual in Nivkh culture. The legend tells of a woman who married a bear in human form. She returned to her village with her bear-children, and the bear was killed by her relatives. Before dying, the bear promised that if people would honor bears in a special ceremony, the bear spirits would provide protection, good hunting, and prosperity. The ceremony involves raising a bear cub in a special enclosure for several years, treating it as an honored guest, before sacrificing it in a carefully ritualized manner. The bear's spirit is believed to return to the mountain god and report on how it was treated. The ceremony, which can last several days, includes elaborate singing, dancing, dog sacrifices, and feasting. It reinforces the sacred relationship between humans, animals, and the spirit world.
