title: The Bamboo of a Hundred Sections
summary: A classic Vietnamese folk tale about a poor, hardworking orphan named Anh (or Khoai) who was taken in by a wealthy but cruel landlord. The landlord promised his beautiful daughter in marriage to Anh if he worked for three years without complaint. Anh faithfully completed his service, but the landlord refused to honor his promise and instead demanded that Anh bring him a 'bamboo of a hundred sections' as a final condition — an impossible task since bamboo naturally has continuous joints without divisions. Heartbroken, Anh went into the forest weeping, where a kind spirit (or a magical old man in some versions) took pity on him and taught him the magic words: 'Join together, join together, bamboo sections, join together.' Anh chanted the words, and the bamboo sections magically fused into one hundred-jointed stalk. When the landlord saw the miraculous bamboo and scoffed at it, Anh chanted the reverse spell: 'Separate, separate, bamboo sections, separate.' At this, not only did the bamboo separate, but the landlord himself was split into a hundred pieces. Anh then married the landlord's daughter, who had always loved him, and they lived happily. The tale is a classic Vietnamese story about the triumph of perseverance, kindness, and justice over greed and deception.
moral: Patience and kindness are ultimately rewarded, and greed and cruelty bring their own destruction.
source: Vietnamese folk tradition, Truyen co tich Viet Nam

title: Bakunawa: The Moon-Eating Serpent
summary: In Visayan mythology, Bakunawa is a massive sea serpent or dragon believed to cause eclipses by attempting to swallow the moon. Originally a beautiful sea goddess, Bakunawa was transformed into a serpent as punishment for falling in love with a mortal. Each night she rises from the ocean depths to chase the moon across the sky. When she catches it, the people must make loud noises with pots and pans to frighten her away, causing her to release the moon. This ancient myth explains lunar eclipses and reflects the deep connection between Filipino ancestors and celestial observation.
notes: Bakunawa is notably absent from creature DB. Should be added as a Philippine dragon/serpent type.
moral: The natural and spiritual world reveal divine truths to those who seek them.

title: Bawang Merah Bawang Putih (Shallot and Garlic)
summary: A popular Javanese folktale of two stepsisters: Bawang Putih (Garlic), who is kind, diligent, and beautiful, and Bawang Merah (Shallot), who is spoiled, lazy, and cruel. After their father dies, Bawang Putih is mistreated by her stepmother and stepsister. While washing clothes in the river, she accidentally loses a garment and meets an old woman who returns it after Bawang Putih helps her with chores. The old woman rewards her with a pumpkin containing jewels. Greedy Bawang Merah replicates the journey but behaves rudely, receiving a pumpkin filled with snakes and scorpions. The story teaches that kindness is always rewarded while greed and cruelty bring misfortune.
notes: The Indonesian equivalent of 'Cinderella' — one of the most widely told tales in the archipelago.
moral: Kindness is always rewarded while cruelty brings its own destruction.

title: Badang the Strongman
summary: Badang is the legendary Malay strongman from ancient Singapura (pre-colonial Singapore) whose feats of strength are celebrated in the Malay Annals (Sejarah Melayu). Originally a slave, Badang gained superhuman strength after consuming the regurgitated vomit of a river spirit — a grotesque but powerful magical substance he had declined to eat before being tricked into it. His strength became legendary: he could lift boats single-handedly, uproot trees, and hurl massive boulders. The Raja employed him as the royal strongman, and he famously defeated a champion from India in a strength contest by lifting an impossibly heavy stone. This stone, known as the Singapore Stone, once stood at the mouth of the Singapore River.
notes: Connected to the historical Singapore Stone, which bore ancient inscriptions.
moral: True honor lives on in stories passed down through generations.

title: Batu Belah Batu Bertangkup
summary: A classic Malay folktale from Perak about a mother who abandons her children after being seduced by a forest spirit. The story follows two siblings, a brother and a sister, left to fend for themselves when their mother disappears into the jungle with a mysterious man. The children wander alone until they encounter a massive split rock called Batu Belah Batu Bertangkup — a sentient stone that opens and closes like a mouth, devouring anyone who passes too close. The sister is swallowed by the rock, but the resourceful brother uses trickery and the help of forest animals to rescue her. The story teaches children about responsibility, family loyalty, and the dangers of trusting strangers.
notes: A well-known Perak folktale with multiple variants. Warnings against parental neglect.
moral: Honor your parents, for ingratitude brings divine punishment.

title: Bukit Shahbandar (The Warning Hill)
summary: A Bruneian legend about Datu Mansur, a corrupt Shahbandar (harbor master) who abused his authority by overtaxing merchants and stealing from the Sultan's treasury. The people suffered under his greed, but none dared speak against him. An elderly village bomoh (shaman) named Nenek Fatimah warned him that the ancestors would not tolerate his corruption. When Datu Mansur dismissed her and increased his oppression, supernatural storms gathered over his hilltop settlement. Lightning struck the ground, the earth trembled, and the entire settlement — including Datu Mansur — was transformed into stone. The hill became a permanent warning to those who abuse power. Today, Bukit Shahbandar stands as a reminder of divine justice and the consequences of corruption in leadership.
notes: Explains the origin of a real geographic feature in Brunei. Moral: corruption leads to destruction.
moral: True honor lives on in stories passed down through generations.

title: Behula and Lakhindar — The Serpent Goddess's Trial
summary: Chand Saudagar, a wealthy merchant and devotee of Shiva, refused to worship the serpent goddess Manasa. In revenge, Manasa killed his six elder sons by snakebite. His last son, Lakhindar, was married to Behula in a specially constructed iron chamber meant to protect him from snakes. But on the wedding night, Manasa sent a snake that entered through a tiny crevice and killed Lakhindar. Refusing to accept her husband's death, Behula placed his body on a banana-leaf raft and floated down the river for nine months, enduring storms and dangers, until she reached the court of the gods. Her unwavering devotion moved Manasa, who restored Lakhindar and all his brothers to life. Chand finally acknowledged Manasa's divinity. Behula is celebrated in Bengali culture as the epitome of wifely devotion.
source: Manasamangal Kavya, Bengali medieval epic (13th-18th centuries)
moral: Unwavering devotion moves heaven and earth

source_references.title: The Serpent's Bride (Manasamangal Kavya): A Study in Bengali Folk Literature
source_references.author: Dinesh Chandra Sen
source_references.note: Scholarly analysis of the Behula-Lakhindar narrative from the Manasamangal Kavya, documenting its Bengali folk-liturgical origins and snake goddess traditions.

title: Bonbibi – The Lady of the Sundarbans Forest
summary: Bonbibi is the guardian spirit of the Sundarbans mangrove forest, worshipped by both Hindus and Muslims in Bengal and Bangladesh. According to legend, she was sent by Allah to protect forest-dwellers from the demon-king Dakkhin Rai, who ruled the forest in the form of a tiger and demanded human sacrifices. Bonbibi, accompanied by her brother Shah Jangali, fought and defeated Dakkhin Rai, forcing him to agree never to harm those who called upon Bonbibi's name. Before entering the forest to collect honey or wood, locals pray to Bonbibi for protection against tigers, and her shrines are found throughout the Sundarbans. She represents the syncretic folk traditions of Bengal where Hindu and Muslim practices blend seamlessly.
full_text:
In the vast mangrove forest of the Sundarbans, where the Ganges meets the Bay of Bengal in a labyrinth of tidal rivers and mudflats, the line between life and death is drawn by the tiger. Every year, honey-collectors, woodcutters, and fishermen enter this tangled green wilderness knowing that some will not return. The Royal Bengal Tiger claims dozens of lives each year, and the waters hide crocodiles and storms that can swallow a boat whole. But the people of the Sundarbans are not without protection. They have Bonbibi, the Lady of the Forest, and her story is one of the most widely told in all of Bengal.

According to the Bonbibir Gaan, the epic poem that wandering minstrels have sung through the delta villages for centuries, Bonbibi was born in the forest itself. Her mother, Gulal, had been abandoned in the mangroves by her husband Ibrahim, who had been tricked into leaving her by his jealous first wife Phulbibi. Alone and desperate, Gulal gave birth to twins: a daughter, Bonbibi, and a son, Shah Jangali. When Gulal could not care for both children, she took the boy and left the girl in the forest, surrendering her to God's will. But Allah sent a doe to nurse the infant Bonbibi, and she grew up among the trees, wild and divine.

Years later, Ibrahim returned out of guilt and found Gulal and Shah Jangali. He offered to take them home. But Bonbibi, now a young woman, reminded her brother of the reason they had been born in the forest. They had been sent to protect the people of the Sundarbans from Dakkhin Rai, the demon-king who ruled the southern reaches in the form of a great tiger. Dakkhin Rai was the son of the sage Dandabakhya and the goddess Narayani, and he claimed dominion over all the mangroves, demanding human sacrifices from anyone who entered his territory.

Bonbibi and Shah Jangali traveled to Medina to receive their divine orders, then crossed the Ganga and arrived at the edge of the Sundarbans. They called out the azan, the Islamic call to prayer, and its sound echoed through the mangrove canopy. It was a warning to all who lived in the forest: a new power had come.

Narayani, Dakkhin Rai's mother, was the first to challenge them. She demanded that only a woman should fight a woman, and she and Bonbibi clashed among the tidal creeks. Bonbibi prevailed, but instead of killing Narayani, she offered her friendship. "Let us rule this land together," she said. Narayani accepted, and from that day the forest was divided: the northern half belonged to Bonbibi and the people, the southern half to Dakkhin Rai and the tigers.

But the most famous episode of the Bonbibi legend is the story of Dukhe, a young orphan boy. A honey-collector named Dhona needed fifty-six men for a forest expedition and recruited the twelve-year-old Dukhe, the son of his late friend. Before the boy left, his mother warned him: "In the forest lives Bonbibi, the protector of all. If you are in danger, call her name."

The expedition entered the deep mangrove but found no honey. That night, Dakkhin Rai appeared to Dhona in a dream and demanded a terrible price: sacrifice the boy Dukhe, and the forest would yield its treasures. If Dhona refused, all his men would be killed by tigers. Tempted by greed, Dhona agreed. He led the party to Kendokhali, where the trees hung heavy with honeycombs, then ordered Dukhe to stay behind. As the boats sailed away, the boy realized he had been abandoned.

Alone and terrified, Dukhe remembered his mother's words and prayed to Bonbibi. She appeared before him, radiant and calm. "Do not fear," she told him. "Dakkhin Rai will not harm you." When the tiger-spirit lunged from the undergrowth, Bonbibi struck him down, and Shah Jangali chased him across the rivers and through the jungle. Dakkhin Rai fled to the house of Barha Khan Gaji, a Muslim saint who lived at the forest's edge. Shah Jangali pursued him there, and the three returned before Bonbibi for judgment.

Gaji argued that Dakkhin Rai should be forgiven. He reminded Bonbibi that she had once called Narayani her friend, which made Narayani's son her own kin. Unable to refute the logic, Bonbibi pardoned Dakkhin Rai, who in return promised to give Dukhe seven cartloads of honey, wax, and silver. Dukhe was carried home safely on the back of Sekho the crocodile, Bonbibi's faithful servant. When he arrived, he found his mother blind with grief. Bonbibi cured her, and mother and son were reunited.

Today, the Bonbibi tradition remains a living faith throughout the Sundarbans. Before entering the forest, honey-collectors recite her name and leave offerings at small shrines marked by red cloth tied to mangrove roots. The Bonbibi Puja, held each year on the full moon of Magh (January-February), draws thousands of devotees. The Bonbibir Pala, a dramatic performance of the legend, is still enacted in villages across the delta, with no written script needed because every performer knows the lines by heart. Bonbibi represents something rare in the religious landscape of South Asia: a deity worshipped equally by Hindus and Muslims, a figure of unity in a region too often divided by faith. She does not promise dominion over nature. She promises coexistence.
source: Bonbibir Gaan (oral ballad tradition of the Sundarbans)
moral: True honor lives on in stories

source_references.title: Bonbibi: The Lady of the Sundarbans
source_references.author: Ramkantha Mitra
source_references.note: Early scholarly documentation of the Bonbibi cult of the Sundarbans, recording oral traditions of the forest goddess worshipped by Hindu and Muslim communities alike.

title: The Ballad of Mahua — Forbidden Love in Bengal
summary: One of the most famous ballads of the Maimansingha Gitika, Mahua tells the story of a young Brahmin man named Naderchand who falls in love with Mahua, a beautiful girl from the much lower Kaibarta (fisherman) caste. Despite the rigid social hierarchies of 18th-century Bengal, the two lovers pursue their forbidden romance. The ballad depicts the tension between individual desire and societal norms, with vivid scenes of Bengali village life. Mahua's beauty, the secret meetings by the river, and the ultimate tragic consequences of their cross-caste love resonate with universal themes of love against social convention. The ballad is notable for its lyrical beauty and its unflinching portrayal of the pain caused by caste discrimination in traditional Bengali society.
full_text:
The Mahua ballad is one of the crown jewels of the Maimansingha Gitika, the great collection of Bengali folk ballads from the Mymensingh region of eastern Bengal. It was written by the minstrel Dwij Kanai and transcribed by Chandrakumar De in the early twentieth century from the oral performances of village singers in what is now Netrokona district, Bangladesh. When the folklorist Stella Kramrisch first read the ballad in Dinesh Chandra Sen's English translation, she wrote: "In the dizziness of fever I saw Mahua, Nader Chand, and Humra as if in a vision. Whatever I have studied of Indian literature, I have not come across any story so touching, so simple and so fascinating."

The story begins in a village on the banks of a river in Mymensingh, where Naderchand, a young Brahmin man of good family, first sees Mahua. She is the daughter of a Kaibarta — a fisherman or rice-cultivator of the lower castes — and her beauty is spoken of throughout the district. Naderchand, educated and from a family of priests, should have had no interest in a girl from so far beneath his social station. But the ballad treats his falling in love not as a transgression but as a force of nature, as inevitable and unstoppable as the river that runs through the village.

Their courtship unfolds in secret, by the river and in the mango groves at the edge of the village. The ballad's poet paints these meetings with extraordinary tenderness — Mahua's dark hair catching the light, Naderchand waiting in the shade of the jackfruit tree, the sound of temple bells mixing with the cry of kingfishers over the water. But the secrecy is itself a kind of violence. In the rigid caste hierarchy of medieval Bengal, a Brahmin man courting a Kaibarta girl was not merely taboo — it threatened the entire social order. A Brahmin's ritual purity was believed to be contaminated by contact with lower castes, and a marriage across such a divide would have brought shame upon both families.

The ballad does not romanticize the lovers' defiance. It shows the real cost. When the relationship is discovered — whether by a jealous neighbor, a suspicious relative, or the simple impossibility of keeping a secret in a village where everyone watches everyone else — the consequences fall hardest on Mahua. Naderchand, as a Brahmin man, has some social protection. Mahua, as a lower-caste woman, has none. The ballad portrays the village's reaction not as a single act of cruelty but as a system closing in: the whispered conversations, the averted eyes, the gradual exclusion from communal life.

The supernatural world of the ballad is woven into the daily life of the village, as it is in all the Maimansingha gitikas. The serpent goddess Manasa, whose worship was central to the riverine communities of eastern Bengal, is present in the background — her temples stand at the village edges, and her festivals mark the turning of the seasons. The makara, the ancient water-spirit that guards the rivers, lurks in the deep pools where the fishermen cast their nets. And the pet-nat, the ghost of someone who died suddenly or violently, haunts the riverbanks where the living and the dead are never quite separated. These beings are not ornaments on the story. They are part of the world in which Naderchand and Mahua live, a world where the natural and the supernatural are separated by a membrane thinner than the surface of the water.

The ballad's climax is not a single dramatic event but a slow unfolding of consequences. The lovers are torn apart by the very society that raised them, and the ballad does not offer the consolation of a tragic deathscape in which love is purified by suffering. Instead, it shows something more painful: love surviving in a world that refuses to let it exist. The poet's voice is lyrical and unsparing, and the ballad's reputation rests on its ability to make the listener feel both the beauty of the love and the cruelty of the world that destroys it.

The Maimansingha Gitika, as a collection, reflects the complex social landscape of pre-colonial and early-colonial Bengal — a world of caste hierarchies, religious syncretism, patriarchal power, and the stubborn persistence of human desire. The Mahua ballad, more than any other in the collection, captures the tension between individual feeling and collective law. It has been performed by pala-gaans for centuries, and its words and melodies still echo through the villages of Mymensingh, where the river carries the memory of a love that the world would not allow.
source: Maimansingha Gitika (Eastern Bengal Ballads), collected by Chandrakumar De
moral: Love transcends death but society's cruelty destroys it

source_references.title: Bengal Popular Culture: The Mahua Ballad Tradition
source_references.author: Sumanta Banerjee
source_references.note: Study of folk ballad traditions of Bengal including the Mahua narrative of caste-transgressive love, preserved through all-night village performances (palagan).

title: Bhil Origin Story — The Birth of the Bhil People
summary: The Bhil tribe, one of India's largest Adivasi groups, traces its origin to the god Mahadev (Shiva). According to the myth, Mahadev was roaming the forest when he created a bow and arrow to teach humanity survival. The first Bhil was born from the sweat of Mahadev's brow. Another version tells that the goddess Parvati created the first Bhil woman from her combings, and Mahadev created the first Bhil man from his nail parings. The bow and arrow (dhanush and teer) is central to Bhil identity — every Bhil is traditionally a skilled archer. The legend of Bhilna Dev and the tiger, in which a Bhil hero tames a man-eating tiger using only his bow and his wits, remains a central cultural story. The Bhil goddess Bhadbhavi is the protector of forests and is invoked before hunting expeditions.
source: Bhil oral traditions, central and western Indian tribal folklore
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

source_references.title: The Bhils: A Study
source_references.author: K.S. Singh
source_references.note: Ethnographic study of Bhil tribal communities including their origin myths, creation narratives, and oral traditions documenting the eponymous ancestor Bhil.

title: The Black-Necked Crane of Bhutan
summary: In Bhutan, the black-necked crane (Thrung Thrung Karmo) is revered as a sacred bird and believed to be the soul of virtuous monks returned to earth in elegant form. Legend says the cranes circle the Gangteng Monastery three times in a clockwise direction upon their arrival in the Phobjikha Valley each October — a traditional Buddhist circumambulation (kora) that is seen as a blessing. The Sixth Dalai Lama, Tsangyang Gyatso, wrote a famous poem: 'Oh white crane, lend me your wings, I'm not going far and away.' Local farmers believe that the arrival of healthy crane numbers promises good harvests, while a decrease foretolds leaner years. The cranes are thought to understand the traditional astrological calendar, timing their migration accordingly. The annual Black-Necked Crane Festival (November 11) features schoolchildren in black-and-white costumes performing the crane dance. Killing a crane carries a sentence of life imprisonment.
source: Royal Society for Protection of Nature (RSPN) Bhutan, Bhutanese folk traditions
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

source_references.title: Bhutan: A Cultural and Pictorial Survey
source_references.author: Michael Aris
source_references.publisher: Library of Tibetan Works and Archives, Dharamsala
source_references.note: Documentation of Bhutanese cultural traditions including the black-necked crane (grus nigricollis) as a sacred bird in Drukpa Buddhist folk beliefs.

title: Bodu Naga: The Serpent Spirit of the Lagoon
summary: Bodu Naga is a great serpent spirit in Maldivian folklore inhabiting lagoons, reefs, and deep channels between islands. In pre-Islamic Maldives, Nagas were revered as powerful nature spirits associated with the waters that surrounded the atolls. Fishermen offered prayers and small offerings before venturing into waters believed to be guarded by Bodu Naga to ensure safe passage and abundant catch. The entity is part of a broader Maldivian tradition of spirit beings (handi, fureytha, dhevi) that could appear in human form — often as beautiful women or well-built men — to lure humans. The Bodu Naga legend reflects the ancient Dravidian naga worship that existed in the Maldives before Buddhism and Islam, and shares similarities with serpent traditions across South India and Sri Lanka. The nagas were said to have been subdued as Buddhism spread through the Indian Ocean, but their stories persist in folklore about dangerous waters and forbidden reefs.
source: Xavier Romero-Frias 'Folk Tales of the Maldives', Maldivian oral maritime tradition
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

source_references.title: The Nagas: Serpent Cult in Eastern India
source_references.author: Haridas Mukherjee
source_references.note: Ethnographic study of Naga serpent worship traditions across eastern India and Bangladesh, including lagoon and riverine communities' Bodu Naga beliefs.

title: Baron Samedi, Lord of the Dead
summary: Baron Samedi (Baron Saturday) is one of the most iconic lwa of Haitian Vodou, the master of death, tombs, graveyards, and resurrection. He is depicted wearing a top hat, black tail coat, dark glasses, and cotton plugs in the nostrils like a corpse prepared for burial. Often shown as a skeleton, he speaks in a nasal voice and is known for disruption, debauchery, and his fondness for tobacco and rum. As head of the Gede family of lwa, he has the final say on death itself and is also a healer and protector of souls. His consort is Maman Brigitte. Baron Samedi can grant resurrection as well as death, making him a figure of both fear and respect.
moral: Not all that seems beautiful is safe
full_text:
In the Vodou tradition of Haiti, when the dead are to be dug up, the ceremonies performed, and the boundary between life and death negotiated, there is one figure who presides over all: Baron Samedi, the Lord of the Dead.

Baron Samedi is not a figure of terror. He is a figure of irreverence, humor, and dark charm. He appears dressed in a black tailcoat, a top hat, dark glasses, and white gloves — the formal attire of a gentleman who has seen too many funerals. His face is painted to resemble a skull, his voice is gravelly and profane, and his laugh is so loud it can shake the walls of the cemetery.

Baron Samedi is the guardian of the dead. He watches over the crossroads between the world of the living and the world of the spirits, ensuring that the dead remain where they belong and that the living do not cross the boundary without permission. When someone is near death, Baron Samedi appears at the crossroads, waiting.

But Baron Samedi is also the protector of children. He guards them from evil spirits and malevolent forces, and he is invoked in ceremonies to ensure their health and safety. He is a figure of paradox — the lord of death who loves life, the guardian of the grave who delights in celebration.

During Vodou ceremonies, when the drums begin to play and the spirits descend, Baron Samedi is often the first to arrive. He dances with impossible energy, drinks rum, tells bawdy jokes, and generally behaves as though the afterlife were one long party. His presence reassures the living that death is not something to fear — that on the other side of the grave, there is laughter.

Baron Samedi reminds the Haitian people that death is not the end, that the dead live on in memory and spirit, and that the best way to honor the departed is with the same joy and irreverence with which they lived.

title: Bijan and Manijeh
summary: Bijan, a knight of Iran and son of Giv, is sent to drive wild boars from Armenian fields. Afterward, the envious knight Gorgin tempts Bijan to cross into enemy Turan by describing the beautiful gardens of Afrasiab. Bijan meets Manijeh, the daughter of Afrasiab, and they fall in love. She drugs him and smuggles him into her chambers, but Afrasiab discovers them and chains Bijan in a dungeon. Manijeh is exiled to wander the wilderness. The Iranian king Kay Khosrow sees Bijan in his crystal cup and sends Rostam disguised as a merchant into Turan. With Manijeh's help, Rostam frees Bijan and returns him to Iran, where Manijeh becomes his wife.

source_references.title: Shahnameh: The Persian Book of Kings
source_references.author: Dick Davis (trans.)

source_references.title: The Epic of the Kings: Shah-nama, the National Epic of Persia
source_references.author: Reza Kaboli (trans.)

full_text:
Bijan was the son of Giv, one of the great knights of Iran, and he was young and proud and very beautiful, and it was this beauty that would nearly cost him his life.

He was sent to drive the wild boars from the fields of Armenia, and he performed this task with ease, for Bijan was the finest archer in all the kingdom and his sword had tasted the blood of a hundred beasts. But after the work was done, he lingered, and a jealous knight named Gorgin whispered to him of the gardens of Afrasiab, the king of Turan, and of a woman who walked there whose beauty surpassed anything Bijan had ever seen or dreamed.

Bijan rode across the border into enemy territory, and in Afrasiab's gardens he met Manijeh, the king's own daughter, and she was indeed as beautiful as Gorgin had described, and more. They looked upon each other and something passed between them that neither could name or resist. Manijeh drugged Bijan's wine and smuggled him into her chambers in the palace, and for a time they were happy in the way that people are happy when they know they are in terrible danger and cannot bring themselves to care.

But Afrasiab discovered them. His rage was beyond all measure. He had Bijan dragged from the sleeping chambers and chained in a dungeon beneath the palace, in a pit so deep that no light reached the bottom. Manijeh was cast out into the wilderness to wander alone, and the people of the court said she would die within a week.

In Iran, King Kay Khosrow looked into his crystal cup of divination and saw Bijan chained in darkness, and he sent word to Rostam: go to Turan, find the boy, and bring him home. Rostam disguised himself as a merchant and crossed the border with a caravan of goods, and in the ruined countryside he found Manijeh, gaunt and sunburned but alive, living on roots and the charity of travelers. She told him where Bijan was held, and Rostam went to the palace and freed the young knight from his chains, and they fled through the desert with Afrasiab's riders at their heels.

They reached Iran in safety. Bijan married Manijeh, and the wedding was the grandest that the kingdom had seen in a generation. And Gorgin, the jealous knight who had started it all, was banished, for Kay Khosrow said that a man who sends his brother to danger for the sake of envy is worse than any enemy across the border.

title: Bahram Gur (Bahram V, the Hunter King)
summary: The Sassanid king Bahram V (Bahram Gur), renowned for his love of hunting (especially onager, giving him the epithet 'Gur' meaning onager), is the subject of numerous Persian legends. His exploits include hunting with a lion and an onager as companions, his romance with the Indian princess Sapinud (whom he marries after killing a lion and a dragon), and his building of the seven pavilions for his seven princesses (the subject of Nizami's Haft Paykar). Bahram embodies the ideal Sassanid king: martial prowess, justice, and cultural patronage. The story of 'Bahram and the Dragon' recounts his killing a dragon to save a beautiful maiden.
full_text:
They called him Gur, which means onager, for he loved the wild ass of the Iranian desert more than he loved the comforts of his palace, and he would ride for days across the burning sands with his bow across his knees and his eyes scanning the horizon for the flash of a tail or the dust of a fleeing herd. Bahram, son of Yazdegerd, was the fifth of the Sassanid kings, and the poets say he was the finest hunter who ever lived.

His hunts were legendary. He kept a lion named Sheroz as a companion, and the lion and the king would ride together across the desert, the beast trotting at the horse's flank like a great golden dog. In one famous tale, Bahram encountered a lion and an onager locked in combat, and he drove his lance between them, separating the two, and then he killed them both with a single arrow apiece, for Bahram never needed a second shot. The court poets competed to describe his marksmanship, and one, the Indian envoy, declared that Bahram could split a hair at the distance of a shout.

But Bahram was not only a hunter. He was a king, and the best of kings, and the stories say that he dispensed justice with the same precision he brought to the hunt. He married seven princesses from seven kingdoms -- Indian, Roman, Chinese, Slavic, and others -- and for each he built a pavilion of a different color, and each pavilion was a masterpiece of tilework and carved stone, and in each he spent one night of every seven, listening to the stories and music of a different culture. Nizami would later write of these pavilions in the Haft Paykar, making Bahram the symbol of the ideal Persian ruler: one who combines the warrior's courage with the sage's wisdom and the artist's soul.

The Dragon tale was perhaps his most celebrated exploit. A maiden, chained to a rock at the edge of a lake, wept as a dragon approached to devour her. Bahram rode between them and fought the beast alone, and when the dragon was slain, the maiden threw herself at his feet and begged him to take her as his wife. But Bahram said he had come to save her life, not to claim her body, and he set her free and rode away, and the poets said this was the measure of a true king -- that his courage was matched only by his restraint.

title: The Berber Creation Myth
summary: In the beginning, the universe was vast empty darkness. Within this void existed a supreme being known as Amun or Anzar, the embodiment of perfection with the power to create and destroy. First, the sky and earth were formed in perfect harmony—the sky personified as a male deity, the earth as a female. From their union, water was born, giving life to the barren world. The supreme being then created various deities to govern aspects of nature—the sea, mountains, wind, fertility, and war. Finally, humans were crafted from the earth itself, molded by the hands of the gods and given the breath of life. Humans were considered part of the natural world, living in harmony with the earth and venerating the gods who governed the forces of nature.
source: folklore.earth/culture/berber/; snip Gods and Monsters - Amazigh Mythology
source_references: Westermarck, Edward A. Ritual and Belief in Morocco. 2 vols. Macmillan, 1926; Basset, René. Contes populaires berbères. Journal asiatique, 9e série, 13: 399–440, 1899
full_text:
In the beginning, there was only Amun—the supreme being, the embodiment of perfection, who existed in the vast empty darkness before the sky had a name or the earth had a shape. From the void, Amun called forth the sky and the earth, and they were made in the image of a man and a woman, bound together in a union so complete that neither could exist without the other. The sky pressed down upon the earth with the weight of starlight, and the earth received the sky with the patience of stone. From their embrace, water was born—the first river, the first rain, the first moisture that would give life to all living things.

Then Amnum created the lesser deities, each charged with governing a piece of the world: the god of the sea who stirred the waters into waves, the goddess of the mountains who lifted the peaks above the clouds, the spirit of the wind who carried seeds across the valleys. War came too, for Amnum understood that creation without conflict would be static and lifeless. Finally, from the red clay of the earth and the breath of the sky, Amnum shaped the first humans—small, mortal, and profoundly connected to the land from which they were formed. The Berber say they are children of both earth and heaven, bound to the Atlas Mountains by the same clay that shaped their ancestors. This is why the mountains remain sacred, and why every Berber village, no matter how small, maintains a shrine to the forces that made them.

title: The Bennu Bird and the Primordial Mound
summary: The Bennu bird—the inspiration for the Greek phoenix—was a self-created being associated with the sun god Ra and the city of Heliopolis. According to the Heliopolitan creation myth, before the world existed there was only the dark, watery abyss of Nun. From these primordial waters arose a mound of earth—the Benben—the first dry land. Upon this mound perched the Bennu bird, a heron-like deity who, with a cry, broke the primeval silence and initiated creation. The Bennu's call marked the beginning of time. Alternatively, the Bennu emerged from the flames of a sacred persea tree in Heliopolis. Each morning at dawn, the Bennu renews itself, representing the daily rebirth of the sun. It was associated with the cycle of life, death, and rebirth, and with Osiris as lord of resurrection. The Egyptian word for the Benben stone relates to the verb 'weben' (to rise), linking the Bennu to the rising sun. Later adapted by Greek writers as the phoenix, the legend spread throughout the Mediterranean world.
source: Sacred Texts; snip Pinch - Handbook of Egyptian Mythology
source_references: Faulkner, Raymond O. The Ancient Egyptian Book of the Dead. British Museum Publications, 1985; Hornung, Erik. The Ancient Egyptian Books of the Afterlife. Translated by David Lorton. Cornell University Press, 1999
full_text:
Before the first sunrise, before the first voice broke the silence of eternity, there was only Nun—the dark, formless, infinite ocean that filled the space between what was and what would be. From within this primordial water, a mound of earth rose of its own accord, as if the universe itself had exhaled. This was the Benben—the first dry land, the sacred mound at Heliopolis that marked the beginning of all creation. Upon this mound there perched a great heron-like bird, radiant with fire and crowned with a crest of feathers. This was the Bennu, the self-created one, the lord of the rising sun.

The Bennu opened its beak and cried out, and the sound shattered the silence of the void. Time began. The sun rose for the first time, and the world unfolded like a papyrus scroll. Each morning at dawn, the Bennu renews itself, rising from the flames of the sacred persea tree in Heliopolis, its feathers catching the first light of day. The Egyptians saw in this bird the promise that death is not the end—that from the fire of each sunset, a new dawn will be born. The Bennu was the herald of Osiris, the lord of resurrection, and its cry echoes through the Egyptian afterlife as the call to eternal life. The Greeks, who encountered this bird in their travels to Egypt, wove it into their own mythology as the Phoenix—the immortal bird that dies in flame and rises from its own ashes, a symbol of renewal that has survived across millennia and across the boundaries of every culture that has heard its song.

title: The Book of the Dead: The Weighing of the Heart
summary: Central to ancient Egyptian funerary mythology is the judgment scene where the deceased's heart is weighed against the feather of Maat (truth and cosmic order) in the Hall of Two Truths. After death, the soul (the ba) journeys through the underworld (Duat), facing dangerous demons, fiery lakes, and gatekeepers. The climax is before Osiris, Lord of the Dead, enthroned with Isis and Nephthys behind him and the forty-two assessor gods before him. Anubis, the jackal-headed god of embalming, weighs the heart on a scale while Thoth, the ibis-headed scribe, records the verdict. If the heart balances with the feather, the deceased is declared 'true of voice' and is allowed to enter the Field of Reeds—a paradise of eternal life. If the heart is heavier with sin, it is devoured by Ammit, the 'Devourer of the Dead'—a composite creature with the head of a crocodile, the torso of a lion, and the hindquarters of a hippopotamus—and the soul ceases to exist. Spells from the Book of the Dead were placed in tombs to guide the deceased through this judgment.
source: Pinch - Handbook of Egyptian Mythology; snip Sacred Texts
source_references: Faulkner, Raymond O. The Ancient Egyptian Book of the Dead. British Museum Publications, 1985; Hornung, Erik. The Ancient Egyptian Books of the Afterlife. Translated by David Lorton. Cornell University Press, 1999
full_text:
The Egyptians prepared for death with the same meticulous care they gave to life, for the journey through the Duat—the underworld—was the most dangerous voyage a soul could undertake. When the body was sealed in its coffin, the ka and the ba—the spiritual double and the soul-bird—slipped free and began the passage through the darkness. The underworld was a landscape of terrors: rivers of fire that burned without consuming, gates guarded by serpents with knives for teeth, and demons who demanded the secret names of the gods before they would let the traveler pass. The deceased carried spells inscribed on papyrus—chapters from the Book of the Dead—each one a key to unlock a door or repel a monster.

At last the soul arrived in the Hall of Two Truths, where Osiris sat enthroned in deathless green, his face serene, his crook and flail crossed upon his chest. Before him stood the scales of Maat, the goddess of truth and cosmic order. Anubis, the jackal-headed embalmer, placed the heart of the deceased upon one side of the scale. Upon the other he laid the single white feather of Maat—the measure of a life lived in justice. Thoth, the ibis-headed scribe of the gods, stood ready with his reed pen to record the verdict. If the heart balanced with the feather, the soul was declared maat kheru—true of voice—and was led by Horus into the Field of Reeds, a paradise of endless harvests and cool rivers where the dead lived forever in the company of the gods. But if the heart was heavy with sin, it was cast to Ammit, the Devourer—a beast with the head of a crocodile, the chest of a lion, and the hindquarters of a hippopotamus—and the soul ceased to exist entirely, its name erased from the book of eternity.
